### drezy — 2018-08-03T22:09:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/after-9-months.20448/post-247414>

> [WalterNL said:](/goto/post?id=247408)
>
> [https://rl.se/sub-solar-point](https://rl.se/sub-solar-point)
>
> Didn't really know where to post this, thought [@drezy](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/19879/) would enjoy this. At this moment, people in Yucatan are enjoying some good UV with the sun directly overhead.

Very cool.

There are some really cool software libraries out there just like the pyephem(mentioned in your link). I actually still owe [@JanSz](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/933/) some analysis on the sun's [airmass (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Air\_mass\_(astronomy))](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Air_mass_\(astronomy\)) in his location versus his ancestor's. If it's not punctual enough for him I think it's pretty fair to blame him... for having crazy friends like me in the first place.

Embedded in latitude is both solar and magnetic details. I can visualize solar data like this:

On the left is my the airmass for the sun in my latitude versus a lower Canadian latitude for mid December. Without getting into the science at all there is a lot of low hanging fruit right there aesthetically. My location gets a longer low-airmass (aka more direct sunlight) period in the winter than Canada. No duh, right? There are two things that I hadn't thought about until I saw it:

1.  the middle of the solar presence (14:30/2:30pm for me vs 13:00/1:00pm for my Canadian friends)
2.  The grey "sunrise-y" section is more drawn out/protracted for the Canucks. I hadn't really thought about that before. That early morning red -->UVA -->UVB transition would take place over a longer period during the day chosen...

Peppers:
I'm dry hanging my cayenne peppers for some spice during the rest of the year. It looks something like this:

Every morning as I sit under the peppers a humming bird swoops in and stares at the string of brightly colored objects. I've got him stumped. I noticed that when they fly 4 inches from your face they are louder then you'd think and that little sharp beak looks more dangerous too. It's got my respect.

Morning light for another win:
My child has always been a little poo deferrer. Never full blown constipation or anything like that, just a hesitation to get 'er done. For over a decade I've served the function of listening to breathing or watching body motion and saying "Hey, I think you may have to poo. Why don't you just go sit and see..."

Now, no longer an issue. 35+ minutes shirtless in the morning sun and I can set a clock by the regularity now.

Just like we need to sleep within every 24 hours, we could need the equatorial sun every x number of months.

### drezy — 2018-08-04T15:24:23+0300

One consult and my mom got an estimate straight from doc for her condition in that environment. It was very simple and clear, but she sadly never followed through. I did get her to get 30 minutes morning sun every morning. Maybe that buys some time.

While on downer mom news:
My wife is currently in China at the bedside of her mom who is on full life support for close to a week. Machines are breathing for her and a balloon in pumping/assisting her heart. There have been no signs of consciousness that I heard of.

This is a situation where there is no real training to be a good husband. Both my wife and her father were academically inclined so they were kept in schools and rushed off to build careers since they were very young. Many women in my family were caretakers for handicapped and elderly people. I got to see from a young age that people just plain die and that's that. My wife and father-in-law never saw as much of this process up close. This is going to be a huge change for my wife and her dad. I'm at a loss for how to help.

### Phosphene — 2018-08-04T17:53:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/after-9-months.20448/post-247474>

Sorry to hear of the maternal woes. I’m fortunate and have been spared this so far for the most part but of course the sickle will eventually slash. I’m pretty sure this is the reason I’ve stayed emotionally distant. I used to believe I would completely lose my shit when close family died, and feared for my sanity. Yogic philosophy totally eased my fear of death but I’m still not looking forward to it.

I understand the tradition of care taking and it’s deeply ingrained in me. I’ve distanced myself from family and friends on my personal quest the past few years but the pull is still strong and I will of course be there when truly needed. So hard to reconcile all of this with the looming 5G doomsday—living in the now but still preparing for the future.

I used to mock the preppers...

The sickle is so unpredictable.
Most of my married life I was (self assured) that my DW will burry me.
That was giving me comfort.
All I had to do is to plan for her comfort after I am gone.
She was very far from being mitochondriac but she was 6 years younger.
Assuming that women have about 2 years longevity edge over men, I had 8 years edge over her.
Statistically likely true, it did not worked in my case. (Black Swan, but Taleb's swan not Jack's).

I think when I will start looking for another woman, her longevity estimate will be a major criteria point in my selection.
I would rather be alone than go thru the process again.

……………..

She was a very determined and driven lady that grew up in a poorer province and eventually became a dean at a college in the country's capitol. She was always a force to reckoned with. I was not on her favorite list until about 8 years ago when she fell and injured herself. I carried her up 3 flights of steps in her non-handicapped building. I'm unable to explain why, but since then I always get thumbs up and clapping from her when we meet or Skype. I think it's slightly annoying to my wife who tore through several university degrees in order (at least in part) to gain basic approval and all I did was carry her up some stairs and I get the clapping.

She was the central figure of the family and will be missed when she goes.

I've made shrimp boils 4 times in the last week. It's high DHA and spilling the food out onto a butcher paper wrapped tabletop feels like a guy thing to me.

Like the picture below (not mine) I include some corn and potatoes for my son since he's got one decade old heteroplasmy rates.

The clean-up is heavenly easy too:

1.  Gather table wrap and throw in garbage
2.  Wash one pot.

### Erin Gaiser — 2018-08-06T22:00:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/after-9-months.20448/post-247727>

> [drezy said:](/goto/post?id=228111)
>
> After 9 months here I want to summarize my experiences.
>
> I started down this path in late January 2017 by noticing 1/2 mile noon time walks were ripping fat off my frame at a ridiculous rate. Instead of taking it for granted I decided to look that gift horse in the mouth.
>
> Searching for "weight loss sun health" leads right here. Doc and his style on podcasts was amusing and seemed to make some sense but it all just seemed too over the top at the time.
>
> I read a bunch of the forum and blogs where things did get laid out pretty clearly. I noticed many members partially compliant to the recommendations and succeeding or not. Reduce EMF, eat epic-paleo, get sunlight, and get cold still just seemed way too good to be true so I decided I was going to go 100% in for 3 months, prove it wrong, and move on. I had good health so I just went all in.
>
> Results:
>
> -   My main health issue BP is now regularly down 20 points (both systolic and diastolic). MTHFR folks take note. I'm 1298 +/- and 677 +/-. I should be coming unhinged, right? This type of turnaround @ 40+yrs old is very significant to me. Statistically it's 5+ years more life right there.
> -   Now 35 lbs less fat. At a taller height some of that weight could hide well but not all. There is also a noticable estrogen depletion/testosterone boost.
> -   More muscular frame practically no workouts (1 set kettle bell swings, 1 set squats with baby weights on, and 35 pushups total in the 9 months.)
> -   Deep heel cracks are gone
> -   "clicky" knee joints are now silent. I'm back to 20 year old knees.
>
>
> Routine:
> Morning sun always
> Cold tub 45-52F 3 times per week
> 30-90 min Solar noon sun 2-3 times per week
> Grounding ever chance I get
> More oysters in 2 weeks than I used to eat in a year
> Radically reduced emf
> Only red lights after dark
>
> I really thought that all of this was over-the-top hyperbole in March. I decided to become more compliant than most members seemed to be at the time. I bent pretty much no rules. I walk away from this experiment mentally stunned, but physically way further ahead. I just got about 2 decades of vitality back in such a short period of time. It all sank in when a manager I worked with 15 years ago said: "WTF! You haven't f\*cking aged! It's just creepy!" He added "If you ever want back in promise call me first." Apparently in certain tech fields illnesses and surgeries are actually becoming common enough to have to become part of regular scheduling. Turns out trying to live a "regular" life and what @[Barry](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/717/) is doing seem to be meeting in the middle. Modern life has become an endurance sport.
>
>
> I'll follow up with the benefits my family has seen soon.

whats the meaning of heal's cracking?

### drezy — 2018-08-07T22:24:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/after-9-months.20448/post-247863>

My last trip to the coast revealed that my family travel pack was insufficient. The place we stayed at had a windowless (dark) bathroom next to the double twin bed bedroom. Anyone needing to wee wee in the night would blast everyone with light. I ended up finding red tissue paper and wrapped a mini led flashlight I found at a gas station and left it on all night in the bathroom.

I just upgraded my kit to add these red led flashlights and I'm pretty happy with them:
[https://www.amazon.com/gp/product/B01E5EOFSY/ref=oh\_aui\_detailpage\_o00\_s00?ie=UTF8&psc=1](https://www.amazon.com/gp/product/B01E5EOFSY/ref=oh_aui_detailpage_o00_s00?ie=UTF8&psc=1)

It comes in a 3 pack so 1 obviously gets taken by my son immediately, 1 goes in my travel bag, and the last one is in a fixed location in my house.

Food, sex, and friendship are all non-scalable human experiences that should never be messed with, but idiot modern culture loves trying to tinker with it all. I keep finding a small lizard in my cold tub these days.

It's a Mediterranean gecko and it's species hitchhiked quite a long way from [@shiran](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/20240/) and [@Antonis](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/20215/) land. ( [https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mediterranean\_house\_gecko](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mediterranean_house_gecko) )

It must naturally float because it's funny to watch how incredibly sluggish it is when we fish it out of the cold water.

### drezy — 2018-08-14T19:10:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/after-9-months.20448/post-248472>

I've been working on a project with my son. Along trying to teach him to be a good man and evaluate his own meaning of "the good life" I very much wanted to impress upon him the the importance of being an autodidact as well as the ability to take an idea that you can sketch on paper and proceed to manipulate reality until the idea is reality. Ideation to creation is a fascinating little realm in between abstract and concrete.

The process of working with my boy took on it's own life and I decided to push more and more of the responsibilities over to his side, not to speed up the project (cuz it didn't) but to immerse him more in the process.

I woke up on the first day of the 42nd year of my life, test drove my son's latest work and got the best present I will probably ever get, a working UV-A spectrometer with working software to boot:

{ "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

How does it correlate to an expensive NIST calibrated unit?

Looks pretty good to me. (Note the "μ" needed to be a "m" based on some of the math changes).

This works for 365nm and 330nm will be easy to add with minor software changes since the hardware supports it. Next we are shipping a crude alpha unit and software to a member here who has a specific health interest/challenge in gauging his UVA/UVB tolerance.

[@Lahelada](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/6531/) & [@WalterNL](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/19189/) you've asked in the past about this so I'm tagging you in.

Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

With summer travel and some family drama I let myself get thrown off from parts of my routine and put on around 10 useless pounds.

I'm finding it easy to shave off about a pound a day with time restricted eating by sticking to a 9 hour eating window from the first thing I consume other than water (yep I'm counting tea as food and it starts my clock). Of course nothing goes in my craw before the sun has risen. I'm happily surprised at how I am not experiencing any cravings in the evening.

### LieselK — 2018-08-25T17:56:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/after-9-months.20448/post-249381>

I have found that time restricted eating is key for me. I feel so much better and am less hungry if I have a full overnight fast (12-14 hours) and I try to then even restrict water after dinner as well to let my body really rest. Even pregnant, I've maintained a usual 12 hour fast and have had no nausea or cravings. While traveling recently I had a large fast casual meal at 9pm and while it didn't make me feel ill, I woke up the next morning starving! It was very eye-opening.

Sending encouragement to get back to your routine and put yourself first. Everyone benefits!

### drezy — 2018-08-25T18:21:52+0300

While traveling recently I had a large fast casual meal at 9pm and while it didn't make me feel ill, I woke up the next morning starving! It was very eye-opening.

Thanks for the encouragement!

Congratulations on the fresh baby gestating up. That is still a completely perplexing process to me. Sure, I've read much of the science, but how a little romance eventually leads to a whole new person with their own strong opinions popping out still leaves me scratching my head. That along with the fact that this person didn't exist one moment and then the next they are, one way or another, weaved into every primary goal my wife and I have. Wild ride this life business.

I thought about replying to your pork belly question, but a sentence with the word "pork belly" and the word "too much" was unrecognizable as English to me. Surely there must have been a typo.

So do you plan on getting a sonogram sneak peak at baby? If so do you want to know if it's a girl or boy? I haven't studied the sonogram approach around here yet.

### LieselK — 2018-08-25T19:21:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/after-9-months.20448/post-249392>

That along with the fact that this person didn't exist one moment and then the next they are, one way or another, weaved into every primary goal my wife and I have. Wild ride this life business.
>

>
> So do you plan on getting a sonogram sneak peak at baby? If so do you want to know if it's a girl or boy? I haven't studied the sonogram approach around here yet.

Thanks! Kids are fascinating in and of themselves. The more I watch them, the more I see how they inherently know to do better and we just interfere and screw it up. I very much look forward to mornings with a new babe and letting this one be free to do what's optimal. My 3rd kiddo was CTing naked yesterday so I know there's hope!

I have like 6 lbs of pork belly in the fridge right now.

I am conflicted about an ultrasound. It's not optimal. I may go for a quick and efficient anatomy scan because it provides information that will help make everyone at my home birth feel confident in birthing at home. I don't think I want to find out the sex. In the past when we have we always end up with too much CRAP because of lovely hand-me-downs (a definite pro and con). My goal is to keep things simple and just let this new life be a joyful part of our life instead of a huge disruption to our life. It won't need clothes anyway.

Sorry about the moms—had a feeling there. My dad is on the decline again. Getting short of breath and mom cancelled their upcoming trip to Canadian Niagra Falls. I keep telling them they need to recharge their batteries with barefoot grounding in the sun, eat tons more seafood, toss the cabinets full of carby, sugary snacks and gallons of ice cream, wear the blue blockers, turn off the damn TVs. ### Dennis Clark — 2018-09-05T18:02:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/after-9-months.20448/post-250248>

Long ago I came across a 'Peanuts' comic strip that astutely answered the cosmic question, "What is the wonder of science?" The answer, then as now, was, "Where the next grant is coming from." Incremental advancements with predictable results are still the result of kow-towing to the granting system. Journal literature is littered with a lot of junk that doesn't even use the scientific method (acc. to [Karl Popper](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Karl_Popper)). Or, worse, misuses it. Perhaps partial blame can be put on the shoulders of Mr. Spock in the original Star Trek series. He continually mixed up "theory" with "hypothesis." The majority of my colleagues must have been watching, since they are still clueless. On top of that, Ioannidis' work on health research throws poor research design and the inappropriate use of statistics (if that's not redundant) into the mix.

### drezy — 2018-09-07T22:01:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/after-9-months.20448/post-250478>

I happened to be reading an anthropology article from roughly the same period of time Doc Kruse was covering in the latest webinar. These link leads to a paper on Ishi (EEE-shee) the last Yanna American Indian. As the industrialized and electrically lit world Doc described in his webinar was rolling in this article was being written and the details being curated for future mankind:

[https://cloudfront.escholarship.org/dist/prd/content/qt02r6j5s0/qt02r6j5s0.pdf](https://cloudfront.escholarship.org/dist/prd/content/qt02r6j5s0/qt02r6j5s0.pdf)

Some notes:

-   "He would eat no fish the day before the hunt, and smoke no tobacco, for these odors are detected a great way off. He rose early, bathed in the creek, rubbed himself with the aromatic leaves of yerba buena, washed out his mouth, drank water, but ate no food. Dressed in a loin cloth, but without shirt, leggings or moccasins, he set out, bow and quiver at his side. He said that clothing made too much noise in the brush, and naturally one is more cautious in his movements when reminded by his sensitive hide that he is touching a sharp twig. "

The paper didn't mention that the creek was CT temperatures.

-   [@JanSz](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/933/) check out the dude's body. Not skinny not muscular. Slight gynecomastia?

-   "Not only could Ishi call the animals, but he understood their language. Often when we have been hunting he has stopped and said, "The squirrel is scolding a fox." At first I said to him, "I don't beheve you." Then he would say, "Wait! Look!" Hiding behind a tree or rock or bush, in a few minutes we would see a fox trot across the open forest.

After over a year with 3 - 5 days a week working outdoors I see clearly that all these little creatures are for sure communicating amongst themselves and even with their own predators! I've watched a tiny finch attack a hawk for minutes as the hawk tried it's best to evade. I even take orders from a hummingbird 3 times a day as if I were a drive-through fast food worker. The less blooms there are for the hummingbird the longer it complains at me. The more blooms the quieter it is up to even having nothing to say to me when blooms are a-plenty.

### Sheddie — 2018-09-08T04:20:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/after-9-months.20448/post-250511>

> [Dennis Clark said:](/goto/post?id=250248)
>
> Long ago I came across a 'Peanuts' comic strip that astutely answered the cosmic question, "What is the wonder of science?" The answer, then as now, was, "Where the next grant is coming from." Incremental advancements with predictable results are still the result of kow-towing to the granting system. Journal literature is littered with a lot of junk that doesn't even use the scientific method (acc. to [Karl Popper](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Karl_Popper)). Or, worse, misuses it. Perhaps partial blame can be put on the shoulders of Mr. Spock in the original Star Trek series. He continually mixed up "theory" with "hypothesis." The majority of my colleagues must have been watching, since they are still clueless. On top of that, Ioannidis' work on health research throws poor research design and the inappropriate use of statistics (if that's not redundant) into the mix.

That's why I'm a credibility skeptic on \*most\* published research churned out the last 40-50 years (and mainstream media reports when results favor the uber-agenda): [https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Replication\_crisis](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Replication_crisis)
It's not mentioned above but take a second to ponder the 'replication problem' in drug & chemical research -- especially concerns like environmental biodegradability and general 'safety' for flora, fauna, critters and people...

### drezy — 2018-09-10T17:57:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/after-9-months.20448/post-250638>

> [Billybats said:](/goto/post?id=250625)
>

No problem at all. It's beneficial to reman in practice of processing randomness.

> What fat if any do you use with your seafood dishes. I am guessing adding saturated fat once in a while it's the end of the world. .huh. The reason I ask is Dr Kruse said to have mufa's with them but in the video where he makes a dish with oysters he adds I think duck fat but I know he definitely added cream. I also wonder lemon/lime (citrus) may be a big part of the absorption of the fat?

Doc and [@JanSz](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/933/) are more lipodonauts than I am.

Honestly I'm mostly following my hunger these days. This amounts weekly to:
2-3 shrimp boil dinners
2 dozen oysters
3 pork belly dishes (lots of fats there)
optionally 1 fatty ribeye dish

In the heat of the summer I will whip up big shrimp salads with avocado mashed in. I may be behind the lipid curve here.

This winter I'll be looking into using duck fat. My wife will dig that for sure.

Lemons wil emulsify fats and cut your pancreas a break if it needs it.

I will eat a whole bunch of just fat every once in a while as a digestive/pancreatic test. It's highly technical so try to follow.... If I eat a bunch of fat and take a perfectly fine dump then those systems are relatively in order. If instead I crap like a goose then it's time to start paying more attention to those systems. I really try to keep my system able to process a straight blubber diet if needed in case we ever have a walruspocalypse. Cuz I zig when the zobiepocalypse people zag.

### drezy — 2018-09-10T20:25:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/after-9-months.20448/post-250663>

I think it's worth the extra homework to know your supplier and supply chain even in bigger grocery stores.

Along with the knowledge acquired I get VIP treatment at my local seafood counter. Those folks never give me the fish or oysters in the case and always go into the cooler for the freshest shipment.

I know about their personal interests, parents conditions, kid's health, and a handful of long term dreams. I think you have to put some caring in in order to get anything out of a human interaction. I see many people in a rush or just being rude.

### JanSz — 2018-09-12T22:15:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/after-9-months.20448/post-250843>

> [drezy said:](/goto/post?id=250832)
>
> An interesting $0 melatonin boost (at least for mice):
> [View attachment 8839](https://forum.jackkruse.com/attachments/8839/)
>
> Jan you might dig this paper staright from Poland it practically has your name written all over it:
> [http://www.jpp.krakow.pl/journal/archive/02\_11/articles/02\_article.html](http://www.jpp.krakow.pl/journal/archive/02_11/articles/02_article.html)
>
> The bad news for most of us... It looks like being 20 is great for melatonin...
>
> Here we go look at this:
> [View attachment 8840](https://forum.jackkruse.com/attachments/8840/)
>
> Human summer melatonin is lower according to this.

20 is good not only for melatonin.
\------------------------
Autor of the article is using melatonin.
There is variety of ways of taking melatonin that I have newer considered before.
The one I like the best would be similar to a trial where rats were given it in drinking water, so their level was higher throughout the 24hrs.
Not sure how to replicate it and how much melatonin I would have to take over that 24 hrs.
\-------------------
The other, more natural would be to extend the period of darkness.

I could do that, somewhat, by extending my two hours of red light before bed time to 3 hours.
Not sure how pure that situation would be, there are always some other lights if only from time to time.
\=======================================

Human life span, **with or without** modern medicine is around 85-95 years. All living creatures have their inner clock that measures their daily (circadian) and their seasonal (circannual) time. These time changes are mediated by the alteration of levels of melatonin,

Light is blocking the production of melatonin in the pineal gland, darkness is stimulating it.

Finally, the reduction of melatonin observed with aging, may indicate the presence of an "age clock".

Several experiments indicate that fasting in animals substantially increased their production of GIT melatonin.

There is a large group of people taking melatonin daily who believe that melatonin is the "fountain of youth". Those are the subjects which will one day provide an experimental evidence of the efficacy of melatonin.

Chronic nighttime administration of melatonin not only increased the life span of mice but also improved their imunocompetence, increased the weight of their thymus, adrenals and testis and elevated blood concentrations of testosterone and thyroid hormones (25, 26).

The addition of melatonin to the drinking water of middle-aged rats, which produced nocturnal and diurnal concentration of melatonin equivalent to those observed in young rats, reduced intra-abdominal fat and non-fasted plasma insulin and leptin. In addition it significantly stimulated investigative behavior response, which was restored to the youthful levels. So, these rats not only lived longer but also had more fun, without ever going hungry. Interestingly, no changes in hormonal levels or behavior were observed when melatonin was given to young rats (31).

So it can be concluded that if you want to live a very long and healthy life you can either be permanently hungry or instead, you can take some melatonin. That at least a part of the effect of food restriction on the life-span extension is probably mediated via melatonin produced in the GIT and not via the pineal gland was demonstrated by Roky and coworkers (32).

I would like to have a longer and healthy life, but I hate to be hungry (it gives me pain in the stomach). So after what I learned working for almost 35 years in this field, to be on the safe side, I take melatonin occasionally. If nothing else, it gives me nice dreams.

### Sean Waters — 2018-09-13T17:36:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/after-9-months.20448/post-250910>

> [drezy said:](/goto/post?id=250478)
>
> I happened to be reading an anthropology article from roughly the same period of time Doc Kruse was covering in the latest webinar. These link leads to a paper on Ishi (EEE-shee) the last Yanna American Indian. As the industrialized and electrically lit world Doc described in his webinar was rolling in this article was being written and the details being curated for future mankind:
>
> [https://cloudfront.escholarship.org/dist/prd/content/qt02r6j5s0/qt02r6j5s0.pdf](https://cloudfront.escholarship.org/dist/prd/content/qt02r6j5s0/qt02r6j5s0.pdf)
>
>
> Some notes:
>
>
> -   "He would eat no fish the day before the hunt, and smoke no tobacco, for these odors are detected a great way off. He rose early, bathed in the creek, rubbed himself with the aromatic leaves of yerba buena, washed out his mouth, drank water, but ate no food. Dressed in a loin cloth, but without shirt, leggings or moccasins, he set out, bow and quiver at his side. He said that clothing made too much noise in the brush, and naturally one is more cautious in his movements when reminded by his sensitive hide that he is touching a sharp twig. "
>
> The paper didn't mention that the creek was CT temperatures.
>
>
> -   [@JanSz](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/933/) check out the dude's body. Not skinny not muscular. Slight gynecomastia?
>
>
>
> -   "Not only could Ishi call the animals, but he understood their language. Often when we have been hunting he has stopped and said, "The squirrel is scolding a fox." At first I said to him, "I don't beheve you." Then he would say, "Wait! Look!" Hiding behind a tree or rock or bush, in a few minutes we would see a fox trot across the open forest.
>
>
>
>
> After over a year with 3 - 5 days a week working outdoors I see clearly that all these little creatures are for sure communicating amongst themselves and even with their own predators! I've watched a tiny finch attack a hawk for minutes as the hawk tried it's best to evade. I even take orders from a hummingbird 3 times a day as if I were a drive-through fast food worker. The less blooms there are for the hummingbird the longer it complains at me. The more blooms the quieter it is up to even having nothing to say to me when blooms are a-plenty.

This is incredible. I'm still reading through

### Sean Waters — 2018-09-13T17:42:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/after-9-months.20448/post-250911>

> [drezy said:](/goto/post?id=250895)
>
> [@Sean Waters](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/19464/) My son put in his Thursday and Friday lunch order so I just sealed up the Goan Vindaloo I mentioned to you way back. I'm going to have to adapt that for pork belly, In smaller bite sized (high surface area) pieces pork belly in a vindaloo should be musical.

If only I could eat meat and pork man and I'd be doing this! I fucking love the Pork Vindaloo in Goa. The butter chicken is incredible too. Just a few weeks ago 1 week of Beef and Pork literally made me depressed.... not quite there yet with the Shit on the shingle. Still building the redox.

But I love your idea of Fat testing - I seem to tolerate 120g of Ghee or coconut oil per meal, that's about 4 tablespoons of Dry or Cold Fat, and about 8 if it was liquid. I think that's higher than most, but 5 tablespoons or 150g of either seem to be the limit - I either get the shits or I get a really sore or feverish throat. The throat inflamm is probably due to poor redox in my pharynx, I've had Pharyngeal flare ups of Crohn's through my teens before any of the small bowel issues even showed up. Docs used to say "tonsilitis" and give me antibiotics. Fuck me they fucked me up over the years - I used to get an attack about twice a year every year. No surprise Crohn's showed up @ 16 !!!

I'm gonna go back and read your journal from the start it looks like a gold mine of hacks and info.

### Phosphene — 2018-09-13T18:11:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/after-9-months.20448/post-250913>

> [Sean Waters said:](/goto/post?id=250910)
>
> This is incredible. I'm still reading through

Me too. Like I really needed something else to read, but that Ishi paper sucked me in. Damn you interesting people and your diversions!

Loved this (from pg. 8 of the pdf):

“And so departed the last wild Indian of America. With him the neolithic epoch terminates. He closes a chapter in history. He looked upon us as sophisticated children—smart, but not wise. We knew many things and much that is false. He knew nature, which is always true. His were the qualities of character that last forever. He was essentially kind; he had courage and self-restraint, and though all had been taken from him, there was no bitterness in his heart. His soul was that of a child, his mind that of a philosopher.”

To expunge such an influence it is necessary to wash the bow in sand and water.”

### drezy — 2018-09-27T20:37:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/after-9-months.20448/post-251820>

Pulling here from another thread:

> [Denise0918 said:](/goto/post?id=251815)
>
> If 5 G will be coming from satellites I am confused. I was under the impression that the 5G signal could be easily disrupted and needed antenna to be close together for the signal to work. How then can a satellite be useful with such a great distance the signal needs to travel? It does not add up.

It does when you look at the challenges. Distance is a little rough on higher frequencies but trees and other objects are KILLER for them.

True satellites are further away but there are no obstructions. Also let me introduce you to MIMO or plain old beamforming. In the past we had one antenna broadcasting a signal and we got it or not. Now we can use a system of multiple power and packet varied antennas driven by the same "system" and also received by similar multiple receiver antennas. Easier to see the results. Notice the selective noise filtering by using receive beamforming:

What would have been "noisy" signal loss is cleaned up really well.

> [JanSz said:](/goto/post?id=251818)
>
> one reason that some of them work is that they are usually placed between anus and point x within colon, and that area have no or diminished vagal monitoring.
>
> I could use refresher on that topic.
> [View attachment 8988](https://forum.jackkruse.com/attachments/8988/)

If you pushed a hose up my rear I'd claim my vagal tone felt way better prior to the second treatment. Fool me twice shame on me...

I will say that I'd prefer sun on tummy and are and a cold dip. Anymore somewhere between 15-30 minutes of cold and I usually have a small burp in an otherwise burp-less day. Feels like a vagal signal to me that the signal was received.

### JanSz — 2018-10-03T21:05:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/after-9-months.20448/post-252269>

The **foxtrot** is a smooth, progressive [dance](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dance) characterized by long, continuous flowing movements across the dance floor. It is danced to [big band](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Big_band) (usually vocal) music. The dance is similar in its look to [waltz](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Waltz), although the [rhythm](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Meter_\(music\)) is in a **4**
**4** [time signature](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Time_signature) instead of **3**
**4**. Developed in the 1910s, the foxtrot reached its height of popularity in the 1930s and remains practiced today.

I grew up amongst people just like that.

For quite a while I was confused about the "Doc is nasty" comments people love flinging around on the social media site FaceBag.

My grandparents said rougher things than Doc has. Love and caring doesn't always come in a $4.50 Hallmark card with glitter on it.

If you needed someone to stand with you in your corner against adversity I'd bet that lady would scare the shit out of about 75% of adversity and would tooth and nail the last 25% to a bloody mess so it had a good chance to reconsider.

The cat seems to know this without overthinking it with his "I'mma take nap, you got this" attitude.

### drezy — 2018-10-12T02:47:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/after-9-months.20448/post-252945>

Moving forward with a brewery partnership. Went and "sampled the goods" of our favored brewer. Wow, beer sure crosses the blood brain barrier pretty quickly.

I still keep marveling at the cat in the picture [@Phosphene](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/19648/) posted. Due to my family's feistiness the old woman flipping the finger is part of my background and is as interesting as a tree in the background. It's the cat. The cat is fascinating. Look at that cat.

I think the cat knows that it's caretaker may be a little coarse, but it also senses instinctively how deeply it is cared for.

The middle finger is, for sure, a "fuck you", but the fact that the cat, with heightened senses, sleeps though this encounter is total mockery of whatever the threat happens to be in that picture. Not even worth opening the cat's eyes. Massive disrespect!

I'm not a cat person by any stretch of the imagination but I think I could support that particular cat. Free water and circadian matched mice from my property.

### Phosphene — 2018-10-12T15:13:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/after-9-months.20448/post-252960>

Those huge ginger tabby cats are definitely my favorites. They are almost like dogs—loyal and affectionate but tough and territorial. There is an odd genetic thing where this coloration is about 80% male. (And calicos are almost all female. Cat trivia for the day.)

Stray cats have collected me all my life, and the ones I’ve sought out have all been rescues. The orange males are often given up to shelters because they tend to spray to mark territory, even after being neutered (which is automatically done to shelter rescues, for better or worse...)

I’m currently catless for the first time in my life. Lost the last one over the summer. I spent weeks with him in my arms in the sun as he slowly faded. Surprised I haven’t written about it yet.

I’m sure that Babushka loves her cat fiercely, and vice versa. Together till the end.

But yeah, the attitude. All cats have it but that mofo has it in spades.

### drezy — 2018-10-12T19:02:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/after-9-months.20448/post-252980>

> [Mike David said:](/goto/post?id=252973)

> interviewer:why do two magnets repel or attract
> feynman:IDK i cant explain that to you because there is nothing you understand that i can relate that to. they just do.
> i could listen to hours of feynman keeping it real.

Funny, I was thinking about you specifically this morning while listening to something.

I cook or reheat hot lunches for my wife and son and seal them in food thermoses (red pepper flake & turmeric crab & beef stew w/carrots and garden rosemary if you're wondering). This isn't exactly and intellectually engaging process so I put on some audio content every morning.

I very much appreciate getting a glimpse of ways others father. I've never fully subscribed to anything prior to being a father, never quite fit in anywhere, and never cared too much for fitting anyway. We all end up in a pine box(or some equivalent) in the end. Time worrying about social fitting or many other things is truly time wasted.

Fatherhood, though, fits me like a glove. It's an honor for me to be a father and I want to do the best most thoughtful job I can.

It's so odd too. It's not a process that's really under our control very much. It's instinctively driven by and large. It's not like we say :
"Ok honey let me adjust this testicle on the tall dashing son setting. Oh and if you can accelerate an ovary to fire early so you've got two in the oven I'll go ahead and adjust the other testicle for statuesque beach volleyball girl mode for a fraternal twin win-win and lower overall hospital costs."

You also seem to relish fatherhood. I'll absa-fucking-lutely guarantee that the following snippet will up your fatherhood game if you apply it:

Snippet:
[https://youtube.com/embed/GNhlNSLQAFE?start=180&end=412](https://youtube.com/embed/GNhlNSLQAFE?start=180&end=412)

Warning: The retro-velvety audio visual production will take you to a different time and may induce nostalgia

Whole video:

>
> Whole video:

congrats! will give a listen thanks. We as parents incorrectly think we have alot of control over how the kids end up in adulthood. Authoratarianism breeds content the illusion of consent. I used to be an asshole and demand immediate consent. What i realised is when their old enough to do things in the world on their own i want them to be honest with me about what their doing without the fear of god. We sit talk and reason things out. They know when i disagree but they choose their own path. I dont dwell on failure and heap praise and love. I dont sugarcoat reality to protect their phsycy. They know stuff most parents would never tell their kids. I was tested the other day when the nine year old tried to skip school. He hid under the crib after he told me he was going to the bus. I had to wait for the shock and need to strangle to wear off before i handled it. It feel good to know others see me as a good dad that is what i strive for. Damn right i like the life i live because i went from negative to positive.

I realize that it's 2018 and not 1950, but to make it up the ranks in that job as a lady she must have had to deal with some crap.

I've had two lady bosses and got along very well with both. I could, however, see the blood drain from their face when they had to deal with a whiny male teammate. I'm guessing that aversion might be built into many ladies.

Ladies carry an asymmetric burden/risk for sexual activity, have a more sensitive nervous system, have to deal with decades of menstruation, and get stared at and flirted with by guys way below their league, and many of them have convinced themselves that they need to wear really uncomfortable and ridiculously impractical shoes.

I think the least us guys could do is to suck it up a little and cowboy up.

### drezy — 2018-11-08T21:41:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/after-9-months.20448/post-255256>

Woo alert!

Last night before I slept I requested my subconscious (or whatever it is) to help me solve a particular problem I've been wrestling with. Halfway through the night after 3 other dreams I was in a cabin with people I knew but there was a disembodied pest (almost like chasing a hornet around a house). I caught it and immediately realized, right then, that this was a dream state and I needed to haul this up into consciousness level.

That all sounds elegant, but I'm told that, to the casual observer(aka my family), it manifests in the waking world as me shouting "I've got you motherf\*cker!" seconds before waking up and apparently waking everyone else up. I jotted down my notes then (3AM) and they made sense in the morning.

As expected there have been requests submitted for me to hone this process if I want to continue using it. The catchphrase that "It's cool to be like Kekulé" appears to really only have so much mileage...

### ALEXIS TUDOR — 2018-11-16T01:17:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/after-9-months.20448/post-255719>

Sounds inspirational. When you say red light. Are they just incandescent ones you get at any store. I heard UVA lights were good too right?

> [drezy said:](/goto/post?id=228111)
>
> After 9 months here I want to summarize my experiences.
>
> I started down this path in late January 2017 by noticing 1/2 mile noon time walks were ripping fat off my frame at a ridiculous rate. Instead of taking it for granted I decided to look that gift horse in the mouth.
>
> Searching for "weight loss sun health" leads right here. Doc and his style on podcasts was amusing and seemed to make some sense but it all just seemed too over the top at the time.
>
> I read a bunch of the forum and blogs where things did get laid out pretty clearly. I noticed many members partially compliant to the recommendations and succeeding or not. Reduce EMF, eat epic-paleo, get sunlight, and get cold still just seemed way too good to be true so I decided I was going to go 100% in for 3 months, prove it wrong, and move on. I had good health so I just went all in.
>
> Results:
>
> -   My main health issue BP is now regularly down 20 points (both systolic and diastolic). MTHFR folks take note. I'm 1298 +/- and 677 +/-. I should be coming unhinged, right? This type of turnaround @ 40+yrs old is very significant to me. Statistically it's 5+ years more life right there.
> -   Now 35 lbs less fat. At a taller height some of that weight could hide well but not all. There is also a noticable estrogen depletion/testosterone boost.
> -   More muscular frame practically no workouts (1 set kettle bell swings, 1 set squats with baby weights on, and 35 pushups total in the 9 months.)
> -   Deep heel cracks are gone
> -   "clicky" knee joints are now silent. I'm back to 20 year old knees.
>
>
> Routine:
> Morning sun always
> Cold tub 45-52F 3 times per week
> 30-90 min Solar noon sun 2-3 times per week
> Grounding ever chance I get
> More oysters in 2 weeks than I used to eat in a year
> Radically reduced emf
> Only red lights after dark
>
> I really thought that all of this was over-the-top hyperbole in March. I decided to become more compliant than most members seemed to be at the time. I bent pretty much no rules. I walk away from this experiment mentally stunned, but physically way further ahead. I just got about 2 decades of vitality back in such a short period of time. It all sank in when a manager I worked with 15 years ago said: "WTF! You haven't f\*cking aged! It's just creepy!" He added "If you ever want back in promise call me first." Apparently in certain tech fields illnesses and surgeries are actually becoming common enough to have to become part of regular scheduling. Turns out trying to live a "regular" life and what @[Barry](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/717/) is doing seem to be meeting in the middle. Modern life has become an endurance sport.
>
>
> I'll follow up with the benefits my family has seen soon.

### drezy — 2018-11-24T22:09:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/after-9-months.20448/post-256491>

> [Phosphene said:](/goto/post?id=256488)
>
> I thought you mentioned elsewhere that you use candles as well? (Or am I making shit up now?)

If I ever said that I sure didn't stick with it. My bathrooms have a red led plugin night light and my remotest nook in a kitchen has one too. They are not perfect but they are very minimal. Many toes were stubbed, but the cool thing is everyone's night vision has improved.

In the long term I think I'll go with a solar panel that charges batteries that, in turn, run dim red leds at night.

### caroline — 2018-11-25T06:16:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/after-9-months.20448/post-256537>

do you have a smoke house Dan? My Dad built himself one and made and smoked his own sausage. They were unbelievably delicious. I was too disinterested \[aka young and stupid and self-centred probably\] to pay attention at the time but I still remember all the poles of sausage going into the smoke house and the unbeleivable smells emanating.

wonderful memories ....and that is what you are creating with your son.

### drezy — 2018-11-25T15:30:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/after-9-months.20448/post-256558>

I use my dad's and yep, it smells great.

For the tremendous amount of flavor smoke is a cheap ingredient.

This song reminds me of the one and only aerobics class I took in college. Somehow I still like the song. If pop has an element of weird, I can deal with it. Impossible to not start spinning along to this one, though I was reminded that my balance ain’t what it used to be.

### Phosphene — 2018-11-29T18:14:43+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/after-9-months.20448/post-256968>

I have a well loved 23 year old Kitchen Aid mixer from my maternal grandmother. Actually have the sausage making attachment as well, unused of course. (Shocker.)

You may have inspired me to explore this with my dudes over the holidays. About time that thing gets used for something without flour.

### drezy — 2018-11-29T18:28:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/after-9-months.20448/post-256969>

> [Phosphene said:](/goto/post?id=256968)
>
> I have a well loved 23 year old Kitchen Aid mixer from my maternal grandmother. Actually have the sausage making attachment as well, unused of course. (Shocker.)
>
> You may have inspired me to explore this with my dudes over the holidays. About time that thing gets used for something without flour.

Stuffing the sausage is a favorite pastime of mine.

I would encourage anyone new to make a bulk (non-cased) breakfast sausage. Correctly making more technical sausages comes with a metal reward (dopamine hit?), but the first time I made an excellent breakfast patty from scratch is still the most fun I've had.

Let me know if you'd like a recipe.

Also if you get hooked and have a kitchen-aid I cannot recommend this enough:
[https://www.amazon.com/gp/product/B00XLWIA3I/ref=oh\_aui\_search\_detailpage?ie=UTF8&psc=1](https://www.amazon.com/gp/product/B00XLWIA3I/ref=oh_aui_search_detailpage?ie=UTF8&psc=1)

It is pricey but it is 100% stainless steel and such a breeze to clean.

If you move on to casings keep in mind that any kitchen-aid attachment can push about 3lbs before heat starts to be an issue and you're just pushing liquid oils.

### Phosphene — 2018-11-29T18:50:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/after-9-months.20448/post-256972>

Now I remember why the attachment went unused—complicated! But surely worth it. Might start with a simple pate, which for some reason I haven’t made in ages. And the loose breakfast sausage sounds smart as well. Might as well start with a more manageable elephant, and I’d love your proven recipe. Thanks!

Oh, and what do eat this liverwurst with? Surely it needs to be spread on something besides another tongue?

### drezy — 2018-11-29T19:40:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/after-9-months.20448/post-256976>

This dude totally ripped this recipe off from a book I have:
[https://www.splendidtable.org/recipes/maple-bacon-breakfast-sausage](https://www.splendidtable.org/recipes/maple-bacon-breakfast-sausage)

If I had to start somewhere I do that first. Note that it's almost a little piggish b/c it has maple syrup in the sausage. You can exclude it, but if you're looking for a big morale boost on the first try this is a slam dunk.

Over time your tastes adjust. A less sweet version of something like this with ground in cabbage is now the favorite breakfast sausage around my house.

I don't spread it on anything. Way back in my "leaky-gut" discovery days I got fixed on having basic foods with less working parts. I still stick with that now, but for different reasons. Both my parents and my wife's would tell you that breakfast has to be eggs, bacon, toast, and jelly. If you are working on a crappy gut there is zero chance you'll ever sort out your trouble food with that blast.

### drezy — 2018-11-30T01:53:39+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/after-9-months.20448/post-257006>

Good luck out there ladies. The following video is both sad and funny at times much like a human life. It involves sex, women, and implicitly it involves aging. There are no visuals so no need to watch. It's just good listening. It gets difficult to listen to this kind of stuff with our learnings. Hopefully I'm not the only one listening and shouting "C'mon! Aim the cooch at the sun!"

I'll personally vouch that it's worth listening through for the car repair story at the end.

### drezy — 2018-12-03T18:19:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/after-9-months.20448/post-257157>

I've been outed as a cold tubber by describing my weekend trip to gulf swimming at 62ºF as nice and easy. Having brought my family along with me I rarely bother talking about all this much in public unless asked. Then I'll drop a nugget. The lady that sells me an occasional cup of coffee wasn't happy with just one nugget and wants to hear more.

Does anyone have a recommendation for a starter podcast that Doc has done? Video is preferable but not required.

[@JanSz](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/933/) [@caroline](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/888/) [@Phosphene](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/19648/) [@recoen](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/21522/) [@Inna](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/20770/) [@Jenelle](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/18778/) and anyone else?

I admit that many of you have lofty goals of trying to bring many others along. I admire that, but I more and more like the idea of saving the world one me at a time and my work there is well on it's way. The coffee gal has a fresh baby and my stony heart hasn't completely sealed itself off from caring about babies, but I'm working on sealing up that weakness any day now. Let's face it babies are just a 16 year fuse on more traffic.

### recoen — 2018-12-03T18:49:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/after-9-months.20448/post-257162>

The podcast he just did on “what women must know” titled “everything you know about health and healing is wrong with Dr. Jack Kruse” is probably a good place to start!

I seem to be falling into the camp of saving myself first and hopefully being an example to others for them to become interested without forcing it too. Which is interesting because in my ethics class in college we discussed ship, shipmate, self a lot. Basically you should give your life vest to someone else. But I believe now you should put the life vest on then use it to help save others. Because hopefully you are a sane person who will not go quietly into the night and will not lose it when the $ hits the fan. Dr. Jordan Peterson discusses how you have to fix yourself first before having the moral right to “fix” others.

### Sean Waters — 2018-12-04T11:22:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/after-9-months.20448/post-257230>

The Self-Hacked Podcast is one of my favourites.......

He obviously did a load of reading on Jack before the podcast and thought he had it all figured out and was trying to finish Jack's sentences with what he thought he was going to say - but again and again and again the guy just keeps walking into traps and getting it wrong.

Nothing against the Self Hacked guy - He clearly cares about the truth, and about helping himself and others - It actually reminded me a lot of my own journey, and how many times I thought I knew it all and had it down with such confidence - and then the Black Swan Event would smite me from my false throne of knowledge. **This podcast is the Dunning-Kruger at it's finest.**

**Newbies need to see that - they need to see the shear size of the void between what they think they know - and what they actually know.**

The whole podcast is basically Jack teaching that "you can't bio-hack nature"..........

### Jenelle — 2018-12-04T13:31:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/after-9-months.20448/post-257231>

I know in the first few pages of my journal, I shared YouTube videos as I came across them. There are some great ones there, for sure. I know I was all filled up on sunshine & shared my enthusiastic feelings about them openly here.

### Phosphene — 2018-12-04T18:21:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/after-9-months.20448/post-257241>

I second the suggestions so far. There are so many podcasts I hardly remember which I preferred more than others. I’m still a bit flummoxed myself when trying to succinctly introduce folks to these concepts.

I liked the recent woman-focused podcast, though it seemed like just as he was getting warmed up she had to cut it off for her hour time limit. Maybe it’s enough for a newbie but maybe not??

I like [@Inna](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/20770/) ’s simple patient handout here too:

[https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/my-patients-handout.21757/](https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/my-patients-handout.21757/)

Though I did see one possible change needed. Should this say 3-4 hours before bedtime? I know ideally bedtime should be at sunset, but...

“Last meal of the day should be at least 3-4 hours before sunset to preserve circadian rhythms”

I thought [@Scompy](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/17718/) ’s blog with the quiz was quite useful too. Does it need any updates?

[https://jackkruse.com/reality-5-scompys-quantum-quiz-mito-hacking-life/](https://jackkruse.com/reality-5-scompys-quantum-quiz-mito-hacking-life/)

### caroline — 2018-12-06T04:21:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/after-9-months.20448/post-257364>

Tony and I had a pretty rude awakening this past week. We were having a pre Christmas lunch with family as everyone is busy at Christmas.

Someone asked about why we don't always answer our phones ......us " because we keep them on airplane mode and check them periodically"

I never know where my phone is ........so they put me in the stupid and ridiculous and retarded category. As long as we have one phone working so anyone can get us if they need us ....suits us just fine.

Tony attempted to explain why and about nnEMF etc. and the affects. All we got was sneers and massive eye rolling and ridicule - and it went on and on and on. I was tempted to say that I hope you buggers fry. The 19yo granddaughter was particularly mean spirited and her Dad wasn't far behind - even tho I have had a conversation with him about bright lights at nitetime and he seemed to agree and get it at the time.

From now on .....we intend to do what is best and right for us and keep it to ourselves unless someone has an intelligent question.

Even more mean spirited ........they figure that Tony is my experiment - even tho he has always been massively interested in staying healthy and out of Doctor's offices. BTW - Tony is very healthy ....we both are.

Tony has taken all of this on board \[jackkruse.com\] ........ and we are both thriving. We made a massive move north to get a lot more sunshine. We are in a very low population area and we are pretty much surrounded by water and bush. Did we get it completely right? maybe, maybe not but we are prepared to re evaluate and adjust.

We travelled around 900K - 2 days on the road to get there and 2 days home ......just to be royally abused. We will think twice the next time.

We have an 84 year old neighbour who has purchased blueblockers for her and her partner after seeing ours. She is extremely interested in all the things we do for ourselves and why.

Am I bitter? .....yep!

Tony and I look back at our life and we were/are always interested in new and different ideas ....... There is so much we don't know and we are eager to keep learning and following thru ......

### drezy — 2018-12-06T17:12:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/after-9-months.20448/post-257384>

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=257364)
>
> ........so they put me in the stupid and ridiculous and retarded category.

You're neither stupid nor retarded in my book, Caroline.

My son and I had a conversation about the old phrase "Don't kill the messenger!" That is an old phrase for a very good and necessary reason.

> From now on .....we intend to do what is best and right for us and keep it to ourselves unless someone has an intelligent question.

I have not been bowled over by either curiosity or critical thinking in many conversations.

As far as influence I've gotten my wife, son and uncoupled buddy into a cold tub 3 times a week as well as my dad into the sun without a shirt on much more regularly. One other person is starting to ask good questions.

I'll take what I can get but as you can see I'm not tearing up the influence track here. I'm fine with that.

> Even more mean spirited ........they figure that Tony is my experiment

You know I have been thinking you may have enchanted him. But come on! Us guys want to be a little enchanted and influenced. Otherwise where's the fun?

> We have an 84 year old neighbour who has purchased blueblockers for her and her partner after seeing ours. She is extremely interested in all the things we do for ourselves and why.

There is something that would have surprised me in the past. The fact that older folks are more open minded, open, and flexible-- even mentally agile. Now it makes more sense since I know my generation and the following are blue-light toxxed up. Older people have also seen Taleb's Lindy effect more and have seen many things initially peddled as beneficial, revolutionary, or necessary end up causing harm before fading into the history books.

Another old saying : Youth is wasted on the young.

### recoen — 2018-12-06T19:50:52+0200

[@caroline](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/888/) keep putting the life vest on you.

### caroline — 2018-12-08T01:30:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/after-9-months.20448/post-257553>

well .....just to rock my boat a little...........

We have made some very dear and fun friends in our new home/community. I was feeling a little tired yesterday and my gorgeous friend asked me if I was okay. I replied that I hadn't slept very well. My friend told me to get off my phone and computer in the evening!

We have never had this type of convo before so I was pretty surprised by her knowledge about nnEMF and all the disastrous effects.

Now - this will be right up my ally .........her and her partner are having oxy issues - to be discussed at a later time.

Bring it on .........

### drezy — 2018-12-12T01:23:24+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/after-9-months.20448/post-257904>

> [KrusinWitchie said:](/goto/post?id=257900)
>
> iatrogenic... that is a fantastic word! Thanks!

For the bookish that is a concept applied wider than usual in the book AntiFragile by Nassim Taleb. If you like the word, you'll love how the concept is opened up wider in that book.

You'll walk away with blasphemous questions like:
Is more education sometimes harmful?
Is more (or bigger) data sometimes harmful?
and other gems

I have the book and the Audiobook. Many of the concepts in it have helped me swim better in these waters.

I know that [@Barry](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/717/) , [@recoen](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/21522/) , and [@Brent Patrick](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/19843/) (a Phoenican like the above author) are familiar with his work and ideas.

### drezy — 2018-12-16T15:48:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/after-9-months.20448/post-258210>

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=258197)
>
> a keto friendly one .... I guess we could make a nut crust. Sweet potato would be good. There is a great recipe for sweet potato pie from Paul Prudhomme ....is he still alive?

So I missed the reference [@Sun Disciple](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/19565/) brings up here but supposedly pumpkin itself is lower deuterium:
[https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/low-deuterium-carbohydrates.22364/#post-254070](https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/low-deuterium-carbohydrates.22364/#post-254070)

It's fun to cook. With baked goods my running policy is that if anyone in the house wants something we buy a slice from the best bakery we can find that sells it by the slice. Having a whole (or at least minus one slice) pie sitting around is begging for bad decisions to be made.

Do you notice there's a funny monkey calculus that goes on with pie or certain baked goods? There seems to be some kind of inner reasoning that once there's less pie then there's less calories sitting there so it's fine to eat it more frequently. Someone who shall remained unnamed to protect her innocence also compounds this with what I call the vanity slice. The vanity slice is the ridiculously small slice someone takes and convinces themselves, but not anyone else, that they won't be coming back for 4 more vanity slices that end up totaling more than what a regular slice would have been in the first place.

Whatever you choose to do, if you're not going crustless make sure to salt up the crust. Sweet pie filling with a salty crust sure makes a pie shine.

### drezy — 2018-12-16T18:48:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/after-9-months.20448/post-258223>

> [Inger said:](/goto/post?id=258201)
>
> those oysters look awesome!  Wow.. where are there more men like you, Drezy??? Who shuck and serve their wifes oysters?? She is soooo lucky!!

Awww shucks. I don't know what to say....

Wait, no.... There it is. I always have something to say. As far as what make you _**you**_, did you ever hear how people sometimes say "He was product of his environment."? Learning what we learn here really opens the definition of that much more widely doesn't it?

The corporate world was a fascinating zoo of human behaviors [@Inger](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/1057/). I watched people who should be working together to accomplish a project instead subtly obstruct each other. Why? Because each department is generally granted only so much money (per year or quarter)to give out in pay raises during performance reviews. The percentage increase was almost always very small like 1%-2% for the top performers and 0%-0.5% for average performers but just enough, I guess, to elicit funny monkey behaviors. I won't even bother covering the introduction of Kafkaesque peer reviews.

Even that was not the weirdest part. 5 out of 7 of my peers that I started with have been divorced since then. It's shocking seeing what people will do for small gain, like those little raises, while setting up for huge losses like half their net worth not to even mention the relationship and stability for offspring. These we're highly intelligent people.

The peculiar thing about that that really gets to me is that one of my understandings about primates in general goes out the door here. Primates are supposed to guard against losses, even to a fault. I witnessed something odd happen to my peers.

This is interesting and related (the fun part starts around 4 min):
[https://www.npr.org/templates/transcript/transcript.php?storyId=295349615](https://www.npr.org/templates/transcript/transcript.php?storyId=295349615)

In two of the big corps I worked for the group got a mid-grade psych evaluation. Team members gawked at my evaluation. Both evaluations, using similar systems, reported I was "Highly non-compliant". My coworkers considered this a bad thing. I laughed, highlighted that section, and taped the report outside my cube walls, I suppose proving the analysis correct.

That's a long winded way to say that yes I shuck oysters, make all homemade breakfast lunches and dinners, cook favorite dishes, cool or warm cold tubs to requested temperatures, do hair (yep), and several other things like clockwork.

I think you have to be very careful and adult in what you consider important. I was acting on that before I realized it. Any job, with some bonuses, has limited upside. Any close relationship can have large if not infinite downsides if it's not cultivated correctly. If you obsess on limited upside while ignoring large potential downsides, even though it may make you seem "non-compliant", you are a disaster in waiting.

So much of my quirks and observations mesh with the ideas and people here. I'm really glad I found my way here.

### KrusinWitchie — 2019-01-02T22:24:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/after-9-months.20448/post-259354>

> [drezy said:](/goto/post?id=258210)
>
> Do you notice there's a funny monkey calculus that goes on with pie or certain baked goods? There seems to be some kind of inner reasoning that once there's less pie then there's less calories sitting there so it's fine to eat it more frequently. Someone who shall remained unnamed to protect her innocence also compounds this with what I call the vanity slice. The vanity slice is the ridiculously small slice someone takes and convinces themselves, but not anyone else, that they won't be coming back for 4 more vanity slices that end up totaling more than what a regular slice would have been in the first place.

That’s what was called a Silver Sliver in our house. Out of the whole family, the only Silver that wasn’t obese was my grandmother who would fast if she was traveling and would go outside to get her vitaminD. Smart lady!

### drezy — 2019-01-13T18:06:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/after-9-months.20448/post-260234>

Past drezy, remember when you besmirched our good name by conflating the properties of a BEC and fermonic fluid?

[https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.p...of-vermont-2018-are.21424/page-10#post-244979](https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/deep-implications-of-vermont-2018-are.21424/page-10#post-244979)

It turns out you didn't pay enough attention in 11th grade chemistry class when you first learned Pauli's exclusion principle:

The bosons can condensate in the same quantum state! Half spin fermions must occupy different states.

If you forget this lesson ever againWolfgang Pauli will slap you in the face:

Does he look like he'll put up with any nonsense?

### Jack Kruse — 2019-01-16T00:42:24+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/after-9-months.20448/post-260456>

Boson = light = magnetic field has no real effect on it.

Fermion = electrons and protons

Both are affected by magnetic fields but in different ways.

A **fermion** is a particle that follows [Fermi–Dirac statistics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fermi%E2%80%93Dirac_statistics). These particles obey the [Pauli exclusion principle](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pauli_exclusion_principle). Fermions include all quarks and [leptons](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lepton), as well as all [composite particles](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Composite_particle) made of an [odd number](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Even_and_odd) of these, such as all [baryons](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Baryon) and many [atoms](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Atom) and [nuclei](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Atomic_nucleus). Fermions differ from [bosons](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Boson), which obey [Bose-Einstein statistics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bose%E2%80%93Einstein_statistics).

A fermion can be an [elementary particle](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Elementary_particle), such as the [electron](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Electron), or it can be a [composite particle](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Composite_particle), such as the [proton](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Proton). According to the [spin-statistics theorem](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spin-statistics_theorem) in any reasonable [relativistic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Theory_of_relativity) [quantum field theory](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_field_theory), particles with [integer](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Integer) [spin](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spin_\(physics\)) are [bosons](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Boson), while particles with [half-integer](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Half-integer) spin are fermions. So, H+ has a 1/2 quantum spin state and deuterium has a +1 number.

H+ and D have different magnetic moments so they act differently in a magnetic field.

This raises an interesting problem..........

**Does deuterium act like a boson because of its different quantum spin number?**

What does this mean and imply?

How much do you know about OAM?

How does OAM link to energy and information in Quantum Thermodynamics?

### drezy — 2019-01-16T03:19:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/after-9-months.20448/post-260462>

Bonhomme de livre:
Tu devrais dormir

Do not forget to add your half spin states to come to an odd or even count for atoms or molecules.

Prof Wolfgang Ketterle will explain some of the behavior of bosonic atoms here:

[https://www.youtube.com/watch?t=4222&v=0AKdmw7Whn0&app=desktop](https://www.youtube.com/watch?t=4222&v=0AKdmw7Whn0&app=desktop)

Since it's observational science the whole video is worth viewing for anyone delving into the science:

My curiosity is wild but I've tried to be somewhat parsimonious with my Q&A time(I appreciate that many people have more serious issues) and have tried my damnedest to build upon what Doc pointed me to and some extracurricular study. That is what got me to study Halton Arp, a closer look at Dirac and negative energy, Jayant Narlikar, and Ketterle.

### Jack Kruse — 2019-01-16T04:13:34+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/after-9-months.20448/post-260468>

So is a BEC or Fermi-Dirac system a "fractally-based optical ring resonator"?? -----Is life's Mandelbrot set and optical ring resonator??

Do atoms in us absorb light but yet emit a frequency of light that is greater than the light we absorbed?

And if this is so, is this how atoms are cooled to slow down to take advantage of quantum mechanical abilities in light we cannot observe well at macroscopic levels to do things we just do not expect?

Is this why ALL living cells emit ELF-UV light?

At the 11:30 mark Ketterle makes the error I expected him to make.............why is physics in trouble?

How light operates in a lab is not how it works in us.

**WHAT MIGHT LIFE REALLY BE?**

Could it be described as a "fractal optical ring resonator" that does some of the most interesting and unexpected things to light in order to build multiple quantum computers and machines throughout our cells?

The title of the article below is "Physicists Built a Machine That Breaks the Normal Rules of Light".

The article should have read, when you change the environment light is in you uncover non-linear effects around light that life has figured out how to take advantage of for 3.8 billion years. I think physicists perspective of what they think light really is, IS THE PROBLEM today in biophysics. We do not know it all yet about light and the more we learn the better people will do in medicine that uses light as the main prescription to get people well using quantum biology.

Physicists have assumed time-reversal symmetry is how light acts in all environments. Anyone who is alive knows we cannot live our lives in reverse. This should be really solid EXPERIMENTAL proof that you do not understand all the nuances light offers. The arrow time is a reflection of how light operates in a living system when it is captured and used. This is what gives things an arrow of time.

Their first assumption stated is thus, if you were to play a tape of light's behavior forward and then backward, you would see it behave in the same way moving in both directions in time. This is called time-reversal symmetry. Outside of life do we have data that shows non-living systems can alter time reversal symmetry? If we do, this means your perspective about light and CIRCADIAN biology is DEAD WRONG.

When will they learn that cells/tissues are "fractally built optical ring resonators" that contain topologic insulators codified magnetically in DNA that must be hydrated by water made from the mitochondrial matrix to get these effects in the living state? When will they learn that the mitochondrial matrix powers these loops using sunlight?

When will they realize the sunlight becomes a laser used to cool atoms to make a BEC to make a fractal optical ring resonator?

I wonder how long it will take?

[https://www.livescience.com/64490-weird-light-ring.html](https://www.livescience.com/64490-weird-light-ring.html)

### JanSz — 2019-01-16T16:38:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/after-9-months.20448/post-260491>

basic blocks of nature, any object have constituents:
protons, neutrons, electrons

they have even or odd number of constituents

even are called bosons
odd are fermions

but
fermion (pairs) behave as bosons

lithium(6)=fermion
lithium(6) have (3protons, 3neutrons, 3electrons)
{pair of two lithium(6)} = boson

\---
1 electron=fermion
2 electrons=boson-----> this is how superconductivity works

..........

### drezy — 2019-01-17T00:50:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/after-9-months.20448/post-260562>

I'm loving this info and the whole ride. I'm also happy to see the April '16 info reverberating again.

I'm glad I got a golden ticket to the chocolate factory.

My main update to my journal and testament to following Docs bouncing ball is that I rewrote the script for my own life.

A year and a half ago I resonated with an older gentleman who has lots of knowledge but lacked available capital. Thanks to my enhanced perception (3-legged stool shout out) I recognized the situation for what it was and had the balls to take a calculated risk.

I worked on developing a commercial property and spent so much time outside doing manual labor, being my own boss, laying stakes for road crews to follow, measuring septic system designs, and following u with minor repairs.

I just got commitments on all of the properties and have backup offers.

It's been a bigger success than I anticipated and now I am aiming at phase 2 (rinse and repeat) .

The profitability is significantly more than I'm used to in the high-tech world and it's also tax defferable (aka no taxes for rinse-and-repeat maneuver).

So

-   Wife sleep way better
-   Profits are significantly up
-   No time spent in shitty office cubes
-   Much more time outside
-   Less but better human relationships
-   Lower blood pressure
-   Keener perception
-   Crazy vivid and memorable dreams
-   A feeling of belonging (here)
-   Firmer erections (I wouldn't be me if I didn't mention that)

Thanks Easter Bunny (Doc)!
