### Penny — 2017-12-04T20:53:01+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-december-2017-webinar.20531/post-229490>

This is a great article:
[https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC4733494/](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC4733494/)

**Submolecular regulation of cell transformation by deuterium depleting water exchange reactions in the tricarboxylic acid substrate cycle**
[László G Boros](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=Boros%20LG%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=26826644),a,b,c,\* [Dominic P. D’Agostino](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=D%26%23x02019%3BAgostino%20DP%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=26826644),d [Howard E. Katz](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=Katz%20HE%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=26826644),e [Justine P. Roth](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=Roth%20JP%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=26826644),f [Emmanuelle J. Meuillet](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=Meuillet%20EJ%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=26826644),g,h and [Gábor Somlyai](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=Somlyai%20G%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=26826644)j
[Author information ► Copyright and License information ►](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC4733494/#)
The publisher's final edited version of this article is available at [Med Hypotheses](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/entrez/eutils/elink.fcgi?dbfrom=pubmed&retmode=ref&cmd=prlinks&id=26826644)
See other articles in PMC that [cite](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC4733494/citedby/) the published article.
[Go to:](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC4733494/#)
**Abstract**
The naturally occurring isotope of hydrogen (1H), deuterium (2H), could have an important biological role. Deuterium depleted water delays tumor progression in mice, dogs, cats and humans. Hydratase enzymes of the tricarboxylic acid (TCA) cycle control cell growth and deplete deuterium from redox cofactors, fatty acids and DNA, which undergo hydride ion and hydrogen atom transfer reactions. A model is proposed that emphasizes the terminal complex of mitochondrial electron transport chain reducing molecular oxygen to deuterium depleted water (DDW); this affects gluconeogenesis as well as fatty acid oxidation. In the former, the DDW is thought to diminish the deuteration of sugar-phosphates in the DNA backbone, helping to preserve stability of hydrogen bond networks, possibly protecting against aneuploidy and resisting strand breaks, occurring upon exposure to radiation and certain anticancer chemotherapeutics. DDW is proposed here to link cancer prevention and treatment using natural ketogenic diets, low deuterium drinking water, as well as DDW production as the mitochondrial downstream mechanism of targeted anti-cancer drugs such as Avastin and Glivec. The role of 2H in biology is a potential missing link to the elusive cancer puzzle seemingly correlated with cancer epidemiology in western populations as a result of excessive 2H loading from processed carbohydrate intake in place of natural fat consumption.

### Penny — 2017-12-04T21:00:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-december-2017-webinar.20531/post-229491>

from the same article - I'm confused... the metabolites that I've made bold seem to be a huge tell that the reaction involving H2 didn't work because of too much D2 - but does that also mean (last line) that glucose, lactic, glutamine and glycine if ingested will feed cancer if not enough H2 is available? Like that could be a total fail for someone taking glycine or glutamine and I think serine was also mentioned... also, it seems that an organic acids test could - maybe - identify the existence of cancer?

**Deuterium carrying oncometabolites - Consistency and specificity of associations regarding mitochondrial matrix water recycling and cancer**
The shift from genetic thinking towards cancer being a metabolic disease has been due to the application of metabolomics platforms identifying “oncometabolites”. Oncometabolites are intracellular products which accumulate, initiate and maintain uncontrolled cell growth with metastasis. One of the first oncometabolites identified is 2-hydroxyglutarate, a relatively rare metabolite that is found in high concentrations in gliomas \[[34](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC4733494/#R34),[35](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC4733494/#R35)\]. Multiple mechanisms have been suggested for oncometabolites as means of inducing cancer. Altered gene functions and signaling pathways by indirect histonemethylation contribute to oncogenesis. Since the identification of **2-hydroxyglutarate** formed in the cytoplasm, formed from citrate and isocitrate, other mitochondrial products such as **fumarate** and **succinate** in renal cell carcinomas \[[36](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC4733494/#R36)\] and paragangliomas, respectively have been identified. These products accumulate due to the lack of metabolic hydration to form their TCA cycle product, ketoglutarate and malate, respectively, whereby molecular crowding and the lack of metabolic water deriving hydrogen transfer to NADPH by isocitrate dehydrogenase initiate and maintain aggressive tumor growth. Therefore, “oncometabolite” formation is the results of enzymatic defects that recycle low deuterium metabolic water back into carbon cycling, gluconeogenesis and nucleic acid sugar backbone synthesis. The excessive appearance, i.e. accumulation of “metabolically dry” oncometabolites is consistent with our hypothesis that cancer is formed on the basis of mitochondrial defects that lack hydration of TCA cycle intermediates with low deuterium matrix water as the result of such defects. Such claim is supported by the fact that restoring hydratase function of mitochondria reverses tumor cells back to their genetically stable non-proliferating normal phenotype \[[37](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC4733494/#R37)\] with normal matrix water content, composition and morphology. All of the non-TCA cycle deriving oncometabolites arise from aerobic glycolysis, glutaminolysis or one carbon metabolic cycles \[[38](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC4733494/#R38)\] while carrying deuterium from the environment partially through nutrition. These include **glycine** in breast cancer, **asparagine** in leukemia, **choline** in prostate, brain, breast cancers, **glutamine** in myc-dependent cancers, as well as **glucose, serine, lactate and polyamines in most cancers. Common nutrients such as glucose, lactic, amino, glutaminic and glyceric acids become oncometabolites only when mitochondrial ketogenic deuterium depleting and/or metabolite hydrating processes are insufficient to exchange and replace deuterium on specific carbons before these carbohydrate carbon skeletons become functional and structural sugar backbones for DNA, RNA and membrane building fatty acids.**

the common bean reference is interesting - maybe a summer hack?

**Biological coherence of the deuterium loading hypothesis of cancer**
The core reducing equivalent in living cells to produce new DNA and fatty acids via reductive carboxylation and biomolecule synthesis is NADPH and its deuterium loading properties depend on carbon-specific positional glucose phosphate deuterium enrichments, as well as deuterium enrichment of the cytoplasmic and mitochondrial water pools \[[39](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC4733494/#R39),[40](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC4733494/#R40)\]. The above mechanisms are all important because intramolecular deuterium distributions reveal disequilibrium and a chemical shift axis for example in fructose-6-phosphate and glucose-6-phosphate \[[41](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC4733494/#R41)\]. **More specifically, natural glucose source isolated from leaf starch of common bean (Phaseolus vulgaris) or spinach (Spinacia oleracea) is depleted in deuterium in the C(2) position. Carbon specific deuterium depletion in fatty acids from plants \[[42](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC4733494/#R42)\] and other sources \[[43](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC4733494/#R43),[44](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC4733494/#R44)\] is also evident, which generate deuterium depleted matrix water in mitochondria during complete oxidation in complex-IV.** Variations in carbon specific deuterium content of oxidizable substrates in the pentose and TCA cycles point to the biological role of intramolecular deuterium distributions that may ultimately be essential to understand all details of product deuterium abundances in compartmentalized deuterium transferring intracellular systems as oncogenic initiators

### Penny — 2017-12-04T21:11:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-december-2017-webinar.20531/post-229494>

[https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/24157871/](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/24157871/)
**Contribution of serine, folate and glycine metabolism to the ATP, NADPH and purine requirements of cancer cells.**
[Tedeschi PM](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=Tedeschi%20PM%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=24157871)1, [Markert EK](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=Markert%20EK%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=24157871), [Gounder M](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=Gounder%20M%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=24157871), [Lin H](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=Lin%20H%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=24157871), [Dvorzhinski D](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=Dvorzhinski%20D%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=24157871), [Dolfi SC](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=Dolfi%20SC%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=24157871), [Chan LL](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=Chan%20LL%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=24157871), [Qiu J](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=Qiu%20J%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=24157871), [DiPaola RS](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=DiPaola%20RS%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=24157871), [Hirshfield KM](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=Hirshfield%20KM%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=24157871), [Boros LG](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=Boros%20LG%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=24157871), [Bertino JR](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=Bertino%20JR%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=24157871), [Oltvai ZN](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=Oltvai%20ZN%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=24157871), [Vazquez A](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=Vazquez%20A%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=24157871).
**[Author information](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/24157871/#)**
**Abstract**
Recent observations on cancer cell metabolism indicate increased serine synthesis from glucose as a marker of poor prognosis. We have predicted that a fraction of the synthesized serine is routed to a pathway for ATP production. The pathway is composed by reactions from serine synthesis, one-carbon (folate) metabolism and the glycine cleavage system (SOG pathway). Here we show that the SOG pathway is upregulated at the level of gene expression in a subset of human tumors and that its level of expression correlates with gene signatures of cell proliferation and Myc target activation. We have also estimated the SOG pathway metabolic flux in the NCI60 tumor-derived cell lines, using previously reported exchange fluxes and a personalized model of cell metabolism. We find that the estimated rates of reactions in the SOG pathway are highly correlated with the proliferation rates of these cell lines. We also observe that the SOG pathway contributes significantly to the energy requirements of biosynthesis, to the NADPH requirement for fatty acid synthesis and to the synthesis of purines. Finally, when the PC-3 prostate cancer cell line is treated with the antifolate methotrexate, we observe a decrease in the ATP levels, AMP kinase activation and a decrease in ribonucleotides and fatty acids synthesized from \[1,2-(13)C2\]-D-glucose as the single tracer. Taken together our results indicate that the SOG pathway activity increases with the rate of cell proliferation and it contributes to the biosynthetic requirements of purines, ATP and NADPH of cancer cells.

# Thread: Cholesteotoma question?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cholesteotoma-question.20533/>
**Subforum:** Educating Doctors
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jack Kruse — 2017-12-02T00:46:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cholesteotoma-question.20533/post-229300>

Q: [Dr. Jack Kruse](https://www.facebook.com/drjackkruse/?fref=mentions) I've been reading about ear cholesteatomas and endoplasm reticulum and mithocondrial stress, do you have any comment on that?? Thanks.

ANS: Yes, It is very complicated. nnEMF and blue light increase the deuterium fractionation of tissues. Well if you are arounf a lot of nnEMF your ear pays deep attention to this because of how the reflex arc of acoustics works in the brain. You can read about this here: [https://www.jackkruse.com/cpc-14-tinnitus-quantum-view-point/](https://www.jackkruse.com/cpc-14-tinnitus-quantum-view-point/)

I always ask people with this disease about occupation and habits and if they have had tinnitus or a Bell's Palsy. I have never found one patient where this was not true. nnEMF is why I ask because it stimulate growth in the middle ear skin via reflex. You have to know how the acoustic reflex works. Inicdent light forms the afferent loop. This means humans use light to hear as the link above shows. As the nnEMF destroys the reflex arc it allows the middle ear skin to begin to collect massive amounts of deuterium and this stimulates the growth of the middle ear epithelium. Cholesteatoma is a mitochondrial disease and not a genetic one that is mediated thermodynamically by volume changes due to deuterium fractionation in the epitympanum. Throughout the history of oncology research, in both the conventional and alternative cancer research realms, there has been a cause and effect relationship that has been largely ignored. The ability of a cell to divide, whether it be a malignant (CA) or non-malignant cell (cholestetoma), is highly dependent on cell volume (deuterium fraction in tissue growing), as well as membrane potential (DHA).

The collagen tensegrity system is piezo and flexoelectric and releases and absorbs light from the sun diurnally, and this is why cell volume and cancer are related; so when you lose energy and charge in a cell, the cell, mitochondria and nuclear membrane enlarges and the signal heads to the ER via the other mitochondrial membrane to stimulate translation. The temporal sequence of the enlargement inside a cell is the critical difference. Mitochondrial morphology is one of the earliest changes because of changes in how electrons and protons are used in the matrix. Cells that are cycling (dividing), progress through the following phases: G1 (Gap 1) – this is the phase where the cell is preparing for the next phase, which is the S phase, or DNA Synthesis phase. Once DNA synthesis is complete, the cell enters the G2 phase (G 2), where it prepares to enter the final phase, called the M phase, or mitosis.

During mitosis, the cell divides into 2 cells. The cell volume is at its smallest at G1, and gradually increases its volume until it reaches its largest volume in the M phase. This should be intuitive, because the cell must become large enough to divide and then support two cells. This process is stimulated by TCA intermediates loaded with deuterium in this ear disease because nnEMF stimulate deuterium absorbtion in the middle ear. Cholesteatomas are more aggressive and have associated bony and ossicular erosive changes and arise from specific locations in the middle ear because of the volume changes in skin cells. The typical pars flaccida type cholesteatoma arises in the epitympanum. When MRI is used (rare cause CT is better) we can see sigal change in the fat signals when we vary MRI parameters. This is strong evidence for the presence of high deuterium in this area because of its kinetic isotope effect on imaging. It affects image intentsity compared to H+ fat. Deuterium has double the atomic weight the spin effect is the real problem in the middle ear. The numbers on its magnetic moment destroy chemical flux in any system via changes of the TCA cycle in the middle ear from the nnEMF effect of collecting deuterium in this region. This collection of deuterium leads to tissue swelling which stimulate the cell cycle to get to the mitosis phase and skin cells are expert at making ketatin and cholesterol. So the chronic swelling of the cell volumes there turn out the RER and make tons of the stress lipid cholesterol and it is loaded with deuterium. This is a progrowth action because of how volume is controlled. The interesting thing is that young living systems use deuterium (insulin/GH) to retain deuterium to stilmulate growth as we develop. Plants do the same to their fruits because they want birds to eat the fruit to spread the seeds. the fruit is designed to stimulate the birds growth and the growth of the seed to germinate. This is why understanding the nuanace of it is critical fro those interested in biology. Right now bio-chemists are impotent to get that nuance and why everyone has no clue about the etiology of this disease. It mimics cancer and I just did a webinar December 2017 on the mechanism. You might want to watch it at some point.

### NeilBB — 2017-12-04T02:55:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/saturated-fat-cellular-damage.20537/post-229447>

Deuterium labelling is an old technique. But there are many obvious issues in this study. The article is about saturated fats excessively incorporated into membranes being bad, likely that is true. Where it fails is in "assuming" that eating saturated fat is what precipitates this occurrence. It is well known that this is not true. In fact, from a strict dietary standpoint, without even getting into any biophysics, it is easily demonstrated that the best way to increase circulating triglycerides (for potential incorporation into membranes) is to eat a high carbohydrate diet. The liver is expert at converting excess sugars into palmitic acid, a 16 carbon saturated fat. A high fat diet typically does the opposite, facilitating fatty acid oxidation and lowering circulating saturated fats.

I will also say this. Biology has been dealing with deuterium for a long time. Forever, in fact. Higher environmental levels than today even. So I might take a step back and consider other strategies before I would build a distillery to drink DDW 24/7 or sell my house to Qlaravia. Going crazy about the ppm in coffee beans seems like a gigantic waste of time to me. And I don't know that breath analysis of expired water vapor tells you anything about tissue (or more importantly, subcellular) isotopic distribution either. In fact, I seriously doubt it.

And the important distinction between matrix water and other body pools and how they interface seems to have been forgotten here lately as well. Deuterium clearly can be biologically beneficial in the right biological place/time/context as well.

Some studies have also even showed that judicious use of heavy water could prolong life in some animals in some cases. Don't you all wonder why that might be the case? I certainly have been thinking about how that might work for a while, in between jamming and posting rock videos. But that's just me. I'm odd.

**Experimental and clinical evidences of deuterium depletion and cell growth control**
Consistent with the above, the effect of low 2H in water has been shown to control cell proliferation in numerous biological systems _in vitro_ and _in vivo_[\[12,25–27\]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0306987715004399#b0060). _In vitro_studies, in which the only difference of the growth media was 2H concentration in water, confirmed that 2H depletion inhibits cell growth in a dose dependent manner. To the contrary, increasing the 2H concentration over the natural abundance in water stimulates cell growth. The effects observed can be modeled when the growth rate of tumor cells is significantly inhibited in culture prepared with deuterium-depleted water (DDW) and, importantly, that physically restoring 2H levels by adding heavy water restores cell growth rates [\[28,29\]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0306987715004399#b0140)." ### NeilBB — 2018-03-07T15:31:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/saturated-fat-cellular-damage.20537/post-236548>

By deuterium trafficking, I was simply referring to the details of exactly how an organism's cells and organs (a) recognize, (b) sort, (c) possibly utilize, and (d) eliminate and/or compartmentalize deuterium. And, of course, how those processes may be malfunctioning in a modern toxic environment.

It is very hard for me to believe that the body does not normally know how to utilize deuterium. The simplistic idea that Dr Boros uses as an example...that the ATPase nanomotor would "break" every 4.4 seconds if the environmental levels (150-155 ppm) were in matrix water....what he fails to address however is that even with matrix water of 110ppm, the nanomotor would still then break about every 6-7 seconds! Is that so much better? There has to be a lot more to the story...that's all I'm suggesting...cutting edge science is usually hard to apply correctly. Do you remember when leptin was discovered and everyone thought obesity could be cured by just injected leptin?!

### NeilBB — 2018-03-07T18:46:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/saturated-fat-cellular-damage.20537/post-236591>

> [JanSz said:](/goto/post?id=236589)

> how to make DDE, Deuterium Depleted Ethanol.

I've been looking into this for some time, actually, lol. It's not that easy to determine. Conflicting info.

On the one hand, ethanol is almost always naturally fermented by yeast, which involves glycolysis, which in theory should deplete some of the deuterium from the sugar substrates. But on the other hand, it's only yeast glycolysis/fermentation...

Some chemistry research uses deuterium ratios to try to detect adulterated alcohol (and food products) and assume that the products of fermentation should have similar D/H ratios as the substrate carbohydrate it was fermented from. It may or may not be a correct assumption.

Other relevant facts:

Wine grapes, barley (Scotch malt), and rye are products of C3 photosynthesis.

Corn (bourbon) and sugarcane (rum, cachaca) are products of C4 photosynthesis.

Agave (Tequila, Mezcal) is a product of CAM photosynthesis.

Distillation of ethanol/water mix left at cask strength would have to be relatively depleted compared to other preparations.

The water in Scotland is said to be fairly low in deuterium due to the climate.

Do with that what you will and let me know when your distillery becomes operational.

### shannoncorrin — 2017-12-04T19:20:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/led-question.20543/post-229487>

hello,
This is my first time posting a question and I'm nervous, but I felt it was an appropriate one. Being an optometrist, I find this topic of light so fascinating. I have been listening to a few of Leanne Veniers talks. She said that the newer warmer LEDs are not bad to use during the day and have much lower blue light admission. She doesn't advocate wearing blue blockers all day long. Since my exam rooms have no natural light and I'm on a computer, I've been wearing my blutechs while inside, and no glasses outside. I wear them all the time at night and try to keep things dim at home. I just was wondering if anyone had any thoughts on the new LEDs? If using those and f.lux on your computer and nightshift on your phone, does it reduce blue light enough?

Hi welcome
after sunset you can go with the :
Uvex

[https://raoptics.io/](https://raoptics.io/)

During the day if you are under blue lighting, it's best to protect your eyes and skin . Raoptics has good solutions

For the computer a program called IRIS is good

### kris90 — 2017-12-06T14:19:05+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coconut-oil-in-winter.20549/post-229639>

Jack stated recently that CO was a poor choice for winter because the input/current is not strong enough to drive physiological insulin resistance. He mentions the FADH2:NADH ratio is similar to glucose, and the result is a low electron current in the inner mitochondrial membrane that can result in pseudohypoxia and low NAD+.

It's funny I just did a recent, accidental reverse-biohack because I had pretty well cut out CO from my diet in late fall, but I recently added it back in in large amounts with supper because I was losing weight almost too quickly on my ketogenic diet. The past week or so I've been eating about 3-4 tbsp. with my dinner, and I've noticed the following observations:

1) Gained 3 lbs
2) Breathing has become suboptimal
3) Decreased tolerance to the cold
4) Slight increase in hunger levels

Guess I will stick to butter or ghee, and modulate my CO to find the right balance to prevent excessive weight loss. It seems that the purpose of CO is to smother over your carbs in spring and summer to help deplete deuterium.

Anyone else have experience with unfavorable effects of CO in winter?

### Jack Kruse — 2017-12-09T15:08:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/aromatase-estrogen.20551/post-229852>

The SHBG protein, along with albumin (think Dec webinar 2017), are the primary plasma proteins responsible for binding free testosterone and E2 (estradiol) Only 2-3% of the steroids _believed_ to be free and unbound. SHBG is _glycosylated_, which means that it has sugar structures attached to it. This means its levels are very linked to the deuterium fractions inside of our tissues. So sine you embrace the cold live at a high latitude and are taking HCG you can begin to see why yours is high. Glucose is very linked to deuterium fractionation. Why? Deuterium content in SHBG is important in its ability to hold onto the T and E2. These hormones must be held because they control the the action of deuterium deposition in certain tissues to stimulate growth. So their action is quantized.........what limits them? UVA and UVB light is what lowers sex hormone action because the sun depletes us of deuterium. So SHBG must do its job well during daylight to protect the sex steroids from the effect of sunlight.
The average SHBG association constant for testosterone is approximately 1 x 10^9 L/mol, compared to albumin’s 3 x 10^4 L/mol. The **_larger number_** means that SHBG binds its ligand (testosterone) several-fold tighter than albumin. This is because SHBG has more deuterium in it that other proteins and the protein takes full advantage of the magnetic moment spin effect on bonding strength. Its affinity for dihydrotestoterone, or DHT, is higher still because DHT has a shit ton of deuterium in it too because of the ribosome that builds it. To add its fondness of sex steroids, SHBG also holds onto its ligands a lot longer than albumin (Think Dec 2017 webinar) -- up to 20 times. SO your high level tells me you might not be needing all the HCG your taking because you embrace the cold way of life in Canada. This is something you'll have to hack. I also would be concerned that this elevation could affect your affect and cognition and your emotion because of the deuterium effect on the neocortex. Sunlight would really help this out but you dont have it in Ottawa.

As we age heteroplasmy rises that 10% a decade so by nature's recipes we should NEED LESS sex storid hormones so growth stays under control. What can happen when heteroplasmy is high and we use exogenous sex steroids? We can set a tissue up for cancer if th deuterium fractionation is uncontrolled. YOu can control this using glacial melt water to replace matrix water via the oxidative branck fo the PPP. The more tech/blue light you use the more u need to watch your BUN/creat ratio and then decide how much DDW to drink.

This is what if you are going to use exogenous hormones you have to live a deuterium depleted life and most of the mucsle heads don't so they die early because of heart and brain issues because the tissues with the most mitochondria collect the most deuterium fastests.

### Jack Kruse — 2017-12-09T15:45:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/aromatase-estrogen.20551/post-229854>

SHBG-specific cell surface receptor ( R(SHBG)) that binds the molecule in its free form (but not bound to T or E2).The SHBG-receptor complex is activated (or inhibited) by certain steroids (SHBG can bind steroids with affinity equal to its solution state once bound to R(SHBG)) and signals a chain of events that begin with generation of cyclic adenosine monophosphate (cAMP), with the downstream effects being tissue dependent. Here is where all the deuterium fractions becomes big in a cell why? The reality 20 blog showed you a cartoon of the ATPase. cAMP levels are linked to the stoichiometry of the ATPase. I told you that we use about 1500 H+ per second. If you are exercising a ton you need even more H+ to turn the ATPase or get the cAMP signal.

This is why high deuterium fractionation and exercise can kill people. The ATPase cannot spin fast enough to make energy. When that heppens mucle heads drop. It is also why I dont want my fatties doing a shit ton of exercise right away until I get them on 4-6 weeks of deuterium depleting behaviors.

\[qu ote="Jack Kruse, post: 229852, member: 1031"\]The SHBG protein, along with albumin (think Dec webinar 2017), are the primary plasma proteins responsible for binding free testosterone and E2 (estradiol) Only 2-3% of the steroids _believed_ to be free and unbound. SHBG is _glycosylated_, which means that it has sugar structures attached to it. This means its levels are very linked to the deuterium fractions inside of our tissues. So sine you embrace the cold live at a high latitude and are taking HCG you can begin to see why yours is high. Glucose is very linked to deuterium fractionation. Why? Deuterium content in SHBG is important in its ability to hold onto the T and E2. These hormones must be held because they control the the action of deuterium deposition in certain tissues to stimulate growth. So their action is quantized.........what limits them? UVA and UVB light is what lowers sex hormone action because the sun depletes us of deuterium. So SHBG must do its job well during daylight to protect the sex steroids from the effect of sunlight.
The average SHBG association constant for testosterone is approximately 1 x 10^9 L/mol, compared to albumin’s 3 x 10^4 L/mol. The **_larger number_** means that SHBG binds its ligand (testosterone) several-fold tighter than albumin. This is because SHBG has more deuterium in it that other proteins and the protein takes full advantage of the magnetic moment spin effect on bonding strength. Its affinity for dihydrotestoterone, or DHT, is higher still because DHT has a shit ton of deuterium in it too because of the ribosome that builds it. To add its fondness of sex steroids, SHBG also holds onto its ligands a lot longer than albumin (Think Dec 2017 webinar) -- up to 20 times. **SO your high level tells me you might not be needing all the HCG your taking because you embrace the cold way of life in Canada. ### kris90 — 2018-01-12T14:47:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/aromatase-estrogen.20551/post-232321>

@[Jack Kruse](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/1031/) so it's been just over a month since I've stopped my HCG, and have been taking low dose Clomid. Unfortunately, there's been a bit of a dip this time around. Here are some observations:

1) gain of around 2% bodyfat (I'm still quite lean, just went from around 8% to 10%)
2) sleep has not been as deep and more interrupted (still easy to fall asleep)
3) drop in immunity (fighting a cold now that's taking longer than normal)
4) rise in body temps (on HCG was around 35.8 C - 36.5 C, but now I'm 36.5 C - 37.2 C)

All of these changes occurred despite following strict epi paleo ketogenic diet, fasting, AM sun, and I've actually increased my CT to add in ice baths and snow bathing. So it is not as though my environment has changed. Last time I came off HCG, I don't remember crashing as much.

Could HCG really be a major player in my optimal health? I definitely feel like I don't NEED it, but it definitely has some noticeable effects on my wellbeing, including keeping me ripped to the bone without even trying, and enhanced sleep and immunity (probably from the DHEA boost). Would boosting my T levels be detrimental from a deuterium standpoint if it meant better sleep, body composition and immunity with the HCG added? After all, the holy trinitiy is CRON, HCG and cold.

### kris90 — 2018-02-09T18:24:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/aromatase-estrogen.20551/post-234431>

So there have been some great discussions here, and I would like some opinions. I just got my T levels checked while off my HCG:

Testosterone Total: 13.7 nmol/L (6.1-27.1)
Testosterone Free: 235 pmol/L (110-660)
Testosterone Bioavailable: 5.5 nmol/L (2.8-15.5)
SHBG: 43 nmol/L (13-89)
25-hydroxy Vitamin D: 120 nmol/L (75-250)

Now, in terms of my "man-health" I feel totally fine with these numbers. In fact, I've had much lower before. However, I have noticed that my body composition, appetite and sleep have changed a little (for the worse). HCG had me ripped to shreds, never hungry, and sleeping like a baby always. I have gained a bit of fat since coming off HCG, and noticed I eat a little more lately despite doing more CT. Also, my sleep has not been as deep.

Obviously these 3 changes are important, so it makes me wonder if HCG was helping me more than hurting me? I'm still functioning quite well considering my low T levels, but HCG definitely gives me an "optimal" boost, if you will. But if having an extra few lbs of fat, and eating a little more is better for my longevity, then I'll take that. But the sleep has me a bit concerned.

I just realised, my morning ritual of hopping in a cold shower prior to sunrise is probably lowering my T levels. Considering UV light is what lowers sex hormones and it does not show up immediately in sunrise, it would make sense that between sunrise and UV is when sex hormones are the highest, because the balance of blue & red turns on the pituitary gland.

Given that cold (like UV) lowers sex hormones, if I'm using cold before initial sunrise, then I'm probably already lowering T, and making my receptors more sensitive, so when I do go for my AM sunlight exposure, I release less T.

That's probably why my SHBG is up, because I'm clearing T so quickly with my CT. Pretty cool stuff.

I believe Jack's recommendations in his labs are for "warm adapted" humans. I think the reason is because he knows MAJORITY of humans are warm adapted, because we live in a blue-lit/microwaved world and have access to carbs 24/7. SO, if that is the case, he expects to see your hormone levels in the top quartile, because that would indicate you have NOT depleted the compound pharmacy.

Put it this way: if a warm adapted human has low T, then that is problematic. That is likely blue light toxicity/PVN burnout, circadian mismatch, etc. But if you are cold adapted via the ancient pathway Jack speaks of in CT-6 blog, low T is NOT problematic. That was my biggest clue that I was a different animal than the modern human. Which is why comparing my lab numbers to the general makes no sense whatsoever.

### Ted — 2018-04-25T01:45:14+0300

Did you triple your total T and free T? I thought that having a low SHBG is good thing. My total T is high but the free T is low because my SHBG is high and that doesn't allow the T to be free.

### kris90 — 2018-04-25T02:21:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/aromatase-estrogen.20551/post-239880>

> [Ted said:](/goto/post?id=239875)
>
> Did you triple your total T and free T? I thought that having a low SHBG is good thing. My total T is high but the free T is low because my SHBG is high and that doesn't allow the T to be free.

I believe so. I think my total T went as low as 6 nmol/l and Free T below 100 pmol/l because I was so cold adapted. SHBG was up to 84 nmol/l. After Ray Peat my total T hit 21 nmol/l with Free T about 300 pmol/l, and SHBG at 60 nmol/l.

SHBG is not always bad. Low SHBG is typically associated with leptin/insulin resistance and metabolic syndrome, while higher SHBG can indicate the opposite (i.e. low leptin, high adiponectin, metabolic efficiency). SHBG levels rise to protect sex hormones, by binding tightly to them, and can transport them to cells to exert effects on androgen receptors independent of free testosterone. Most people who have high SHBG are living a world of mismatches, so this causes them issues, but for a cold adapted individual, high SHBG is a good thing.

### kris90 — 2018-04-27T16:42:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/aromatase-estrogen.20551/post-240023>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=229733)
>
> Well you said you were losing weight on HCG.......that tells me you dont need it at all. T should cause you to retain deuterium to gain mass.

This post just hit me like a ton of bricks. HCG caused my T levels to go high (above 30 nmol/l). Yet the bizarre thing is that I was losing weight.

T is supposed to cause us to gain weight, which makes sense because back in my bodybuilding days, I used T to bulk up and pack on mass. So if one has high T, and losing weight, then that should be a sign that the androgen receptors are becoming resistant, and now I'm actually FULLY understanding why Jack says I didn't need HCG at all. I was basically driving up my T levels, but my androgen receptors were probably becoming resistant to protect themselves from too much deuterium (hence the T levels stayed in my serum).

Since I've been off HCG, I've actually put on about 5-7 lbs, yet my T levels dropped significantly. This would demonstrate that my androgen receptors are becoming more sensitive, hence lower T levels.

Damn I am understanding hormones at a whole new level! Maybe being shredded @ 7% bodyfat is not as ideal for optimal function as my current 10% state.

### kris90 — 2018-05-15T20:05:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/aromatase-estrogen.20551/post-241261>

Alright ladies and gents, a big win for Jack and his CT 6 blog. I always say I am living proof of his claim with regards to hormone levels in the cold: they do not matter because your receptors become suprasensitized. And now we know that it is a deuterium story.

Here are my latest labs from this morning feeling like a rockstar:

LH 1.2 IU/L 1.0-9.0
FSH 2.9 IU/L 1.0-19.0
Estradiol 37 pmol/L 40-160
Testosterone Total 8.7 nmol/L 6.1-27.1
Testosterone Free Calculated 128 pmol/L 110-660
Testosterone Bioavailable Calculated 3.0 nmol/L 2.8-15.5
SHBG 50 nmol/L 13-89

What is my secret for my LOW T lol?

Spending nearly 12 hours per day in the sun 3-4x per week, CT x2 daily, epi-paleo and drinking tons of cold Canadian water.

I am Canadian, but I look like I'm Cuban with my tan now lol... I expect T levels to rise a little as I pack more carbs in, but right now the ancient pathway dominates, and I feel like a wild animal.

### EStein — 2018-05-16T00:10:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/aromatase-estrogen.20551/post-241300>

> [JanSz said:](/goto/post?id=241287)
>
> DHEA - 3-beta-hydroxy-5-alpha-androst-1-en-17-one
>
> Would you mind locating it for me on this chart, please.
> And how to ask for testing it's level?
>
> [http://www.genome.jp/kegg/pathway/map/map00140.html](http://www.genome.jp/kegg/pathway/map/map00140.html)
>

it's hiding - the common name is **1-DHEA**. What you're taking thinking it's DHEA is actually **5-DHEA**. Which is very difficult to break down into the activated form of DHEA. The DHEA you're taking can increase Estrogen & make you less manly. 1-DHEA doesn't convert to Estrogen.

### Jack Kruse — 2018-05-16T14:50:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/aromatase-estrogen.20551/post-241335>

High estrogen really means one of two things: Not enough sun to lower it on skin or your cells are making too much in the conversion because they have not gotten the same light stimulus below. People forgot what the blogs clearly said. Reality #7 laid it out clearly.

It said, "Sunlight increases sulfated Vitamin D3, histamine, and sulfhydryl groups while lowering (photolysis) adrenaline, steroids, testosterone, estrogen, thyroid hormone, DNA, and RNA. Sunlight is capable of inducing biochemical reactions via photolysis and it induces coordinated endocrine adaptation effects in the eye and the skin surfaces. It affects the sympathetic and parasympathetic systems in the brain by way of the central retinal pathways. The varying AM solar light is the key stimulus for the circadian timing mechanism of the body clock via the central retinal pathways. All these effects are built into the electronics of your proteins under solar power and magnetic flux."

People forget that the eye and skin/fat BOTH have melanopsin. That is what allows the deeper cells to make estrogen when the skin is not getting enough UV light to turn off the FLOW of estrogen in pathways lacking information quanta.

It also said clearly:
He who controls the sun properly can bring blood flow to the choroid surface (RPE) to energize red blood cells and the hemoglobin present in them. This is how phototropism works in the eye. The choroid/RPE/SKIN and FAT mass have many correlates to the phototropism in plant chloroplasts in leaves. The choroid of the eye is primarily a vascular structure supplying the outer retina. The skin has the same construction with most of the arteries just below the surface waiting for the UVA light stimulus to raise the blood vessels to the surface to be irradiated by the sun. Vermont 2017 showed this slide several times and you'll likely see it again this year. The outer portion of the retina is where the photoreceptor melanopsin **is loosely covalently bound to retinol.** All diurnal mammals have this arrangement. Nocturnal mammals have a very strong covalent bond in their systems to deal with different light stimuli to control their scotopic retina and SKIN/FAT.

Melanopsin/retinol controls the neural signaling along the central retinohypothalamic tract and the skin/fat/brain axis in humans. These tracts connect the retina to the suprachiasmatic nucleus (SCN) which controls the ENTIRE circadian mechanism in the body including the one in the gut via the vagus nerve. It does this because it lowers the firing of the PVN by reducing adrenaline and norepinephrine as mentioned above. People forget these very basic connections are present in each and every one of us. Sunlight is the controlling arm of the gut by way of solar exposure of the eye and skin!!!!! It also controls the center that controls how sex steroid hormones all begin and interact with the pituitary. Why?
These tracts continue on and make connections between the retina and SCN to the leptin receptor in the hypothalamus directly. The leptin system, in turn, controls all growth and metabolism of the organism, including all the SEX steroid hormones, because this system controls the circadian release of hormones from the pituitary gland using specific light frequency changes present in AM sunlight. It is the AM blue light (1600K) balanced by the low powered red light that has MORE than 42% of AM light because of the ABSENCE of UVA and UVA on a RELATIVE basis.......
THE TAKE HOME? He who controls the frequency of sunlight via the eye, skin, and subcutaneous fat calls the shots for the anterior pituitary hormones and its effect on human behavior and illness. Sunlight also controls the phototropism in leaves in plants as well as ANIMALS like HUMANS. The universe doesn’t speak with words. It speaks in frequencies and our proteins decipher this Rosetta code to innovate life and it explains why your estrogen is higher. It is time to find your problem in these words because they are buried here. Taking something to lower estrogen is not the answer. Getting more sun in one of these places is the answer.

### Jack Kruse — 2018-05-16T14:52:35+0300

### kris90 — 2018-05-16T15:05:08+0300

Jack the way I visualize the cold working is that it condenses things, and this releases heat. Water surrounds all proteins, and it has to absorb that heat, which shrinks it to maintain the optimal density. I'm assuming this is how the EZ is built as well when water absorbs the IR heat from our tissues, and because cold (and UV) expands the EZ, it excludes D and favors H+

If you can shrink yourself using cold, then you lower your energy requirements, and thus need to produce less hormone. When we shrink our receptor (decrease mass), less energy (hormone) is required to activate it. When we are condensed in the cold, we are constantly dumping heat back to the environment (uncoupling), because we need less. We are reducing entropy?

### kris90 — 2018-05-25T21:48:13+0300

Read QT #8 and posted this:

> So basically, testosterone is an important modulator of the immune system? It helps prevent an over-reaction? So the more sun we get, the more we potentially need testosterone, because UV light lowers it? High T levels are good if Vitamin D levels are optimal? I'm trying to wrap my head around this, because I bathe extensively in UV light (on a bad day, I get about 2-4 hours of sunlight in my eye and skin, and a good day is around 8-12 hours) and I do daily CT, as well as fasting and always blocking blue light indoors. I also use blacklights when indoors for UV-A and used tanning beds throughout winter. The result is EXTREMELY low T (i.e. 8.7 nmol/l or 250 ng/dl). The only possible way I could raise this would be to stop CT, get less sun, stop fasting, and add more carbs to my diet. I just started adding carbs, but I'd say only around 100g. My question is, can we ever get too much sun? I'm just so addicted that it seems out of the question, but I've noticed I've become a lot hairier the last few years of going extreme with sunbathing/sungazing. Do I need to significantly increase my carb intake to help offset my low T levels from all the UV light + cold? I feel pretty good overall, so I'm not necessarily seeking change.

> It seems testosterone is the braking mechanism for apoptosis. I understand UV-A drives apoptosis and this is nature's vaccination for cancer and most mitochondrial diseases. MAJORITY of people today have mitochondrial issues because of bathing in blue light and burying the sun, so I understand the implications. BUT, on the contrary, what if someone like myself is on the other end of the equation: perhaps too much UV-A, and not enough testosterone to regulate apoptosis? It is possible to overdo it as a mitochondriac? I've always felt myself as an outsider and very opposite of most people in terms of my health and wellbeing. I consider myself a few in a million, if not billion. But I'm so ridiculously tanned and never burn, and never get acute illnesses.

Also, when I used to take AAS back in my young and restless days, I noticed one particular cycle I did of test/deca, my immune system was horrid. In those 12-14 weeks, I kept getting ill with viruses: influenza, and 2 stomach bugs. So it makes sense that juicing up with all those androgens would have such a negative effect on my immune function.

Thoughts?

### kris90 — 2019-06-17T20:25:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/aromatase-estrogen.20551/post-272795>

> A second set of studies reported that **SHBG-bound testosterone may be internalized into the cell** through an endocytic process mediated by the membrane protein megalin \[Fig. 4(b)\] (142). According to this proposal, **once internalized, SHBG-bound testosterone is released at the low pH within the lysosome**.

> **Effects of temperature variations**
>
> **Steroid binding is affected by the temperature and may be 2.5 times higher at 4°C than at 37°C** (9, 197, 198). The seminal testosterone-binding experiments were performed at varying temperatures—some studies were performed with ice-cold ammonium sulfate (4°C) (2) or at 25°C (9), which may affect binding equilibrium (Table 1). For example, in a separate study characterizing temperature effects on cortisol protein binding by the equilibrium dialysis method, **raising the temperature from 37°C to 41°C led to an increase of ∼80% in serum free cortisol level** (199).

[https://academic.oup.com/edrv/article/38/4/302/3897170](https://academic.oup.com/edrv/article/38/4/302/3897170)

### kris90 — 2019-06-20T17:11:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/aromatase-estrogen.20551/post-273022>

> **Abstract**
> The ability of hormones to bind to their functional receptors on turtle (Pseudemys scripta) endocrine target tissues in the cold was tested by treating tissues with secretagogues at low temperatures (5-15 degrees) and then following subsequent target stimulation in the absence of secretagogue at a warm temperature (28 degrees). **Administration of thyrotropin-releasing hormone (TRH), corticotropin-releasing hormone, and growth hormone-releasing hormone to pituitaries at low temperatures (20 degrees or below) suppressed responses in growth hormone (GH) and thyrotropin (TSH) secretion and there was little or no response in pituitaries subsequent to warming.** In contrast, gonadotropin-releasing hormone treatment of pituitaries, TSH treatment of thyroid glands, and gonadotropin (FSH and LH) treatment of testes in the cold (down to 5 degrees) was followed by a large response in the target glands (secretion of LH, thyroxine, and testosterone (T), respectively) following warming. **Additional studies with FSH and LH showed that these hormones can bind to testes rapidly (within 5 min) at low temperatures where no acute response is observed, although the dose sensitivity and the extent of this priming in the cold are less than at warm temperatures.** Thus, postreceptor events may be more important than binding per se for temperature effects on hormone responses of tissues, but even this component of cell function varies among tissues. The effects of a receptor-independent secretagogue (tetraethylammonium chloride), which causes cell depolarization by blocking K+ efflux, were also blocked at low temperatures in thyrotropes and somatotropes but not in gonadotropes. **Rapid depressions in TSH and GH secretions following cooling of TRH-stimulated pituitaries and of T secretion in LH-stimulated testes provide further evidence for cold sensitivity of postreceptor processes in these tissues.**

[https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/2289680](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/2289680)

### Malik Alick — 2017-12-07T20:43:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/maliks-journal.20553/post-229743>

Hey everyone,

My name is Malik, 20yrs old male 112lbs, born in Bermuda. Im currently living in Montreal, and studying Jazz Piano Performance. As most members on this forum I've suffered some serious health issues and emotional trauma. My father died two years ago, a month before I got my acceptance letter to my university. He was my main inspiration and mentor musically and really set the foundation for me through the advice and guidance he gave right up until he died. Unfortunately he suffered from high blood pressure and towards the last few years of his life he was on dialysis. Prior to that the HBP had caused aneursym about 15 years ago, which was remedied with a dye that eventually degraded his kidneys. All during this time he was helping my mom to raise me. I never quite understood why sometimes we couldn't go places or do things like playing football/soccer because he would be in bed sick, with gout flareups and just full body pain. Long story short, he is one of the main reasons why I got into health; after seeing him suffer I felt a calling to step up my own health, and ensure a better future for me, and my son to be.
My health issues really started from a young age, I ate cheese sandwiches everyday for school, loved candy, etc. the typical child's SAD diet, which is obviously even worse nowadays. I was underweight and still am to this day; my mom is also very slim and my dad was slim after he became sick. Was very clumsy as a child, had many accidents; split my head a few times, fan into a wall at nursery; i was pretty stupid looking back. I always was fatigued, was a mouth breather, and i wasnt sleeping right. During this time (from age 8) I had picked up the piano after messing around with drums years prior. My father was a musician who recorded in the US as well in my home Bermuda, so I was born into a musical environment. I started off classical trained while also playing in church; genres included gospel, classical (as stated before), r&b, and soul music. I practiced everyday even though I was not a fan of the process; always had a poor work ethic. Maybe that was possibly do to low dopamine among the plethora of other hormonal issues going on with me at that age. Fast forward to my early teens, i was playing in restaurants, concerts and church while still suffering from health issues I tried to block out my health anxiety with my musical pursuits (its a great distractor, probably the best there is). My main issues were digestion; after several bouts with doctors and physicians my anxiety got worse and I started having panic attacks in school, after reading up on various health issues and symptoms. The fast track diet by Norman Robillaird was the first book I read in hopes of finding answers to my health problems and easing my anxiety. It was a success and I started feeling better and became intrigued with improving my own health without the need of a doctor. Other issues came throughout the years, throughout high school and leading up to college all while my dad's health was deteriorating. We worked on a CD together during the last years of his life which is yet to be released (on my to do list).
My mom on the other hand is the complete opposite; very optimistic personality,empathic and worry free attitude. My mom has never been seriously ill in my lifetime and I attribute it to her stress free mentality. Her mother (my grandma) died 1 year before my father, of breast cancer and a bout of dementia right up until she died. My mom states my grandma lived a "healthy lifestyle" and could not fathom the fact she had a cancer diagnosis. I have not fully investigated the EMF levels around my home as there are no building biologists in Bermuda. I am looking to get meters when I have enough money; well aware of EMFs and the role they play through listening to Jack and Emf warrior podcasts. (I have tested my apartment recently here in Canada, and am working to mitigate it with Yshield paint, when I get the money. I will post the results from the consultation if anyone is interested in viewing it)
After, reading various health blogs and articles on different diets, I discovered Jack about 3 years ago but did not seriously get into his material until the last year and a half. His message was definitely than anyone elseI was reading and I sort of shunned most of what I did read early on, only to come back ever since Ive been out here in school. Montreal's just another EMF hellhole as most cities, but much nicer (even more so than my old country), less judgemental and multiculutural. Lots of smokers here but other than those concerns I pretty decent place to live.
Fast forward to today, even though I have so much more toshare, Ive recently purchased a Sperti Fiji Lamp along with UV fluorescents and red light bulbs. Ive been following all the circadian rhyhtm protocols with regards to light, although I don't eat a Paleo diet. Everything I eat is recorded in cronometer for nutritional info. All organic, only sprouted grains, fair amount of vegetables, avocado every day eggs and salmon and sardines every other day mixed with ground turkey and chicken on alternate days.

### Malik Alick — 2017-12-07T21:02:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/maliks-journal.20553/post-229744>

Had a 23andme analysis done a few month ago; 63.8% west african and 25% european with haplotype L3e2a1b1. According to SelfDecode i have higher tolerance for carbs and less tolerance for fats and also I higher susceptibility for heart disease and Alzheimers due to some LPA snps and my APOE E3/E4 allele combo. I recently had some labs done and my vitamin d is 25.64 ng/ml which is extremely low. Aout last year this time I had low t3 mid range t4, lower range testosterone, low DHEA. My diet hasn't changed significantly except for a slightly higher caloric intake and inclusion of more seafood. (I know believe I have some food sensitivities to some of the foods I've been eating repeatedly for months, as Ive had gallbladder pain these last few months during and after meals.) Working on improving diet variation, and including more seaweed to support my thyroid (thought I was getting enough in my diet by recently found out my Himalayan salt contains an almost negligible amount). Perhaps this contributing to my recent worrying spectracell lab results which include:

lp(a): 42.6 mg/dl considered high
HS CRP: 3.69
Insulin:3.8
glucose:77
triglycerides:60
hdl:53
cholestorel:137
ldl:77
non hdl:84
apo b :65
apo a1:145

vldl:24

total hdl: 7410
dense ldl III-188

c peptide: .6

Linoleic acid: 23.9 considered high (possibly due to my daily avocado consumption?)
o6-o3 ratio: 5.7 considered high (i eat flax and chia seeds daily, and pumpkin seeds every other day along side my seafood consumption spread out throught the week)
epa:1.4 low (!!!)
dha:3.6 low (!!!)

As you can see Im pretty inflamed; as of yesterday I brought nordic naturals ultimate omega supplement and have increased my bell pepper intake in hopes of reducing my lp(a) with vitamin c. (Dont know how effective that will be) Despite my vascular inflammation results spectracell still saws my cardiovascular risk is low which is what i dont really understand.
Any reccomendations in what I can do to optimize these labs? I know @[JanSz](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/933/) is experienced with Spectracell and lab interpretations.

To get growth requires swelling and D will cause that for you but at a cost based upon your specific context.

### Wendy Harris — 2017-12-13T21:11:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/maliks-journal.20553/post-230252>

> [Malik Alick said:](/goto/post?id=229743)
>
> Hey everyone,
>
> My name is Malik, 20yrs old male 112lbs, born in Bermuda. Im currently living in Montreal, and studying Jazz Piano Performance. As most members on this forum I've suffered some serious health issues and emotional trauma. My father died two years ago, a month before I got my acceptance letter to my university. He was my main inspiration and mentor musically and really set the foundation for me through the advice and guidance he gave right up until he died. Unfortunately he suffered from high blood pressure and towards the last few years of his life he was on dialysis. Prior to that the HBP had caused aneursym about 15 years ago, which was remedied with a dye that eventually degraded his kidneys. All during this time he was helping my mom to raise me. I never quite understood why sometimes we couldn't go places or do things like playing football/soccer because he would be in bed sick, with gout flareups and just full body pain. Long story short, he is one of the main reasons why I got into health; after seeing him suffer I felt a calling to step up my own health, and ensure a better future for me, and my son to be.
> My health issues really started from a young age, I ate cheese sandwiches everyday for school, loved candy, etc. the typical child's SAD diet, which is obviously even worse nowadays. I was underweight and still am to this day; my mom is also very slim and my dad was slim after he became sick. Was very clumsy as a child, had many accidents; split my head a few times, fan into a wall at nursery; i was pretty stupid looking back. I always was fatigued, was a mouth breather, and i wasnt sleeping right. During this time (from age 8) I had picked up the piano after messing around with drums years prior. My father was a musician who recorded in the US as well in my home Bermuda, so I was born into a musical environment. I started off classical trained while also playing in church; genres included gospel, classical (as stated before), r&b, and soul music. I practiced everyday even though I was not a fan of the process; always had a poor work ethic. Maybe that was possibly do to low dopamine among the plethora of other hormonal issues going on with me at that age. Fast forward to my early teens, i was playing in restaurants, concerts and church while still suffering from health issues I tried to block out my health anxiety with my musical pursuits (its a great distractor, probably the best there is). My main issues were digestion; after several bouts with doctors and physicians my anxiety got worse and I started having panic attacks in school, after reading up on various health issues and symptoms. The fast track diet by Norman Robillaird was the first book I read in hopes of finding answers to my health problems and easing my anxiety. It was a success and I started feeling better and became intrigued with improving my own health without the need of a doctor. Other issues came throughout the years, throughout high school and leading up to college all while my dad's health was deteriorating. We worked on a CD together during the last years of his life which is yet to be released (on my to do list).
> My mom on the other hand is the complete opposite; very optimistic personality,empathic and worry free attitude. My mom has never been seriously ill in my lifetime and I attribute it to her stress free mentality. Her mother (my grandma) died 1 year before my father, of breast cancer and a bout of dementia right up until she died. My mom states my grandma lived a "healthy lifestyle" and could not fathom the fact she had a cancer diagnosis. I have not fully investigated the EMF levels around my home as there are no building biologists in Bermuda. I am looking to get meters when I have enough money; well aware of EMFs and the role they play through listening to Jack and Emf warrior podcasts. (I have tested my apartment recently here in Canada, and am working to mitigate it with Yshield paint, when I get the money. I will post the results from the consultation if anyone is interested in viewing it)
> After, reading various health blogs and articles on different diets, I discovered Jack about 3 years ago but did not seriously get into his material until the last year and a half. His message was definitely than anyone elseI was reading and I sort of shunned most of what I did read early on, only to come back ever since Ive been out here in school. Montreal's just another EMF hellhole as most cities, but much nicer (even more so than my old country), less judgemental and multiculutural. Lots of smokers here but other than those concerns I pretty decent place to live.
> Fast forward to today, even though I have so much more toshare, Ive recently purchased a Sperti Fiji Lamp along with UV fluorescents and red light bulbs. Ive been following all the circadian rhyhtm protocols with regards to light, although I don't eat a Paleo diet. Everything I eat is recorded in cronometer for nutritional info. All organic, only sprouted grains, fair amount of vegetables, avocado every day eggs and salmon and sardines every other day mixed with ground turkey and chicken on alternate days.

Hello - I live in UK but love Bermuda ; going there next week and it's my 45th time ...)
I guess not much of an nnEMF issue there ( 22miles and all ).
I have had the stomach issues you speak of...tried everything, saw 9 gastrointestinal doctors plus tests and scans.
Epi paleo diet for a leaky gut helped me plus I used EC supplement ( digestive enzyme ) by APT in Canada . Made 75% improvement ..
Worth a try ???

### taiyang — 2018-01-13T11:52:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/maliks-journal.20553/post-232406>

"Still struggling with this urination at night..." just a thought which might have nothing to do with your situation: my husband would get up multiple times to pee at night. we thought it was a prostate issue, but there were two other factors disturbing his sleep. when we took care of those factors, getting up 4 times a night dropped to one to no times a night. it's just that the other factors disturbed his sleep which prompted him to maybe in a compulsive way use the bathroom. the two factors disturbing sleep in his case, which don't have to be the same in your case, were mild sleep apnea and drinking 1 to 2 glasses of red wine in the evening. according to the sleep study, whenever he slept on his side, his breathing was fine. the problem was he could not get out of the habit of sleeping on his back. he also was born with a smaller airway. he drank wine to help him sleep, but that actually hurt him by making him sleep less deeply in the middle of the night. now he has a cpap machine and doesn't drink wine. even with an addiction to the game pokemon go and constantly looking at this cell phone, he sleeps well. he used to have headaches waking up, sometimes struggle to stay awake in the daytime, etc. now he is fine.

Then on another podcast heard Ben Greenfield mention more greens, a plant rich keto diet for depleting deuterium. Thoughts?

### Sue-UK — 2017-12-08T16:51:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/deuterium-free-water.20554/post-229781>

I think edible green leaves probably have less deuterium when young, and I've wondered about the chloroplast's ability to keep it out, or maybe the plant keeps it more in the structure of the stalks and less in the leaves, perhaps accumulating over time (plant aging?)....? Or it may be dependent on the water source it grows in? Chlorophyll itself seems to be a bit special for making ATP in mammals ...... [http://jcs.biologists.org/content/127/2/388.long](http://jcs.biologists.org/content/127/2/388.long) _Light-harvesting chlorophyll pigments enable mammalian mitochondria to capture photonic energy and produce ATP._

Still trying to work it out ...

### JanSz — 2017-12-08T17:35:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/deuterium-free-water.20554/post-229784>

> [Danco3636 said:](/goto/post?id=229779)
>
> Any one know if Pelegríno water is naturally lower in deuterium? Or any other water lower in deuterium than others or even a water fitltering system?
>
> Also heard on a pod cast Jack mentioning that not eating green veggies and more meat as greens contains more deuterium and meat less.
> Then on another podcast heard Ben Greenfield mention more greens, a plant rich keto diet for depleting deuterium. Thoughts?

Pelegríno have "normal" amounts of deuterum like any other water including good spring waters like Poland Spring (in my case).

Aquafina (made by Pepsi) have been tested and have 139.5ppm
because
it is RO water and @[Jack Kruse](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/1031/) recommends drinking RO waters
I assumed that all RO waters have about 140ppm.

That assumption may not be very strong,
because latter I got from Cignature Health that:
ground water is between 145 to 151 ppm including RO.

If you want to drink some particular water, that can be tested.

\--------------
Coconut water is on my short hopefull's list.
Jack and Laszlo Boros say that it is good (but no numbers).
...........
I currently dropped Poland Spring, and drink RO water from Costco and coconut water.

..........

### drezy — 2017-12-29T16:29:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/deuterium-free-water.20554/post-231224>

Some of you guys are younger than me so here is a dating analogy.

Imagine a very attractive potential mate causes some minor inadvertent insult to you then feels a bit bad. It's ok because it opens up the possibility of some chat and a date that's maybe even above your station a bit. All indications look really good on the date. Large extravagant dishes like caviar start to get ordered. Later after dropping $500 on drinks and dinner you're standing at the door of your date and you get a quick kiss on the cheek right before the door closes on you. Not horrible, per se, but not what you expected or \*needed\* at the moment...How many more $500 dinners should that date expect from you before you give up on that relationship?

In the above fable:
the "you" = your mitochondria
the minor inadvertent insult = your environment and what likely brought you to this site in the first place
the date (person eating away your money) = you and your choices now that you know what you know
the date event = every decade above ~30 years old
expensiveness of the dinner = the mitochondrial cost of maintaining a less than optimal environment
quick kiss on the cheek = any artificial therapeutic light without much other significant environmental change

### JanSz — 2017-12-29T17:36:08+0200

In my case I was not looking for a keeper.
The keeper found me.
Only much latter I found how much work it was.
After she was done with ballet school she had a job in some town (Lodz).
Then we met on vacations.
For me it was rather non-eventful vacation and I was not expecting to see her afterward.
For her, she quit or newer started her job.
Got a job in my city and started showing up in places I was.
Long story
but the short part
she was a very good wife,
I am always honored to be chosen by her.

I tried one case of this product a few years ago, back when it came in mason jars. It was unpalatable to me at the time, although now I suspect individual toxin loads and perhaps dysfunctional bodily systems may have a great deal to do with subjective taste data. Making the product easier to obtain could go a long way toward giving it a wider evaluation. Personally, if I trusted something to restore my overall health taste wouldn't be an issue. I'd do what it takes, other than "a spoonful of sugar makes the medicine go down." No sugar, please.

### Tanya — 2018-01-14T23:45:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/deuterium-free-water.20554/post-232551>

> [Johnny DeMarco said:](/goto/post?id=232205)
>
> What are your thoughts on the trick of freezing 10% of some RO water then throwing the ice away and freezing again? D2O freezes first, so would be thrown out with the ice. But, in a recent Facebook live with Nathan Walz, I thought I heard the interviewer say a negligible amount of D2O was being removed. Thoughts? What is the Yeti can trick I've heard of?

This is what Langvey said about this process: I used Google translate)
Question: Is it possible to get light water at home?

Answer: You can. The easiest way is to melt the snow or winter ice that fell out during a severe frost. In Moscow, water can thus be obtained with a deuterium content of 137-139 ppm, in Siberia - with a deuterium content of 129-132 ppm, in Yakutia - with a deuterium content of 125 ppm. But at the same time there is no guarantee that you will receive chemically pure water, taking into account that the snow falling near large cities contains a huge amount of harmful impurities.

At the same time, several techniques are described on the Internet, according to which by means of repeated freezing and thawing of ordinary water, it is possible to obtain light water. We are disappointed: our experiments showed that in this way (by repeatedly freezing, thawing and draining) from a bucket of source water, one can get a glass of water, with a deuterium content of only 2-3 ppm lower than in the original water.

I see too many problems with DIVINIA.
I do not know nothing about actual quality of this product,
but the way it is described is lacking big time.
\-----------------------------------------------------------------------
In one place
[https://www.diviniawater.com/science?lightbox=dataItem-iyc52izu](https://www.diviniawater.com/science?lightbox=dataItem-iyc52izu)
it is described as having:
128-132 ppm deuterium level.
That description (true or not) sounds good.
\-----------------------------------------------------------------------
In another place
[https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0787DSJKH?m=AO4POUWNMTMS1&ref\_=v\_sp\_widget\_detail\_page](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0787DSJKH?m=AO4POUWNMTMS1&ref_=v_sp_widget_detail_page)
it is described as
exclusion zone and deuterium depleted water
Functional water - pH balanced, 100% pure (0.00 ppm)

Two problems with that:
implies that it is deuterium 0.00 ppm
it tells that it is exclusion zone water (physically impossible, except for very thin layer on bottle surface).
\-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Somebody who sells this water should at least
clean up the texts everywhere where they are talking about Divinia so it matches.

...............
On top of everything else DIVINIA is super very expensive.
It is over **4x as expensive** as Qlarivia.

### DavidL — 2018-01-17T18:07:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/deuterium-free-water.20554/post-232750>

From the book I recently purchased, the most frequently observed phenomena from consuming DDW:

1\. GENERAL WEAKNESS, DESPONDENCY, DROWSINESS
"Weakenss and drowsiness are attributed to the necrosis of tumour mass and the reactions connected with it."
2\. FLUSH, OCCASIONAL HIGH TEMPERATURE
"Flushing of the face and local skin blush were frequently observed"
3\. TEMPORARY INCREASE OF PAIN
4\. ALLEVIATION AND CESSATION OF PAIN
5\. SWELLING AND SOFTENING OF THE TUMOUR AREA
6\. WARMING OF THE TUMOUR AREA
7\. TINGLING CRAWLY FEELING INSIDE THE TUMOUR
8\. BLEEDING IN THE CASE OF TUMOURS OF THE BLADDER AND RECTUM
"In the case of the two above tumours it frequently happens that necrotic pieces of tissue become detached from the tumour area and leave the body, accompanied by minor bleeding"
9\. INCREASE OF APPETITE AND STRENGTH
10\. WEIGHT GAIN
\* interesting to note that weight gain is one because the actual weight of heavy water is greater than DDW \*
"other physiologic changes evoked by DDW connected to the secondary effect of the thyroid gland"
11\. STOMACH COMPLAINTS
12\. WOUND HEALING AFTER A STRONG DISHCHARGE (WITH OPEN ULCEROUS TUMOURS)
13\. IMPROVEMENT OF THE GENERAL CONDITION
14\. "BRICK POWDER' IN THE URINE
"attributed to necrosis of the tumour, it is advisable to consult a doctor and test blood levels for uric acid"
15\. DECREASE IN SIDE EFFECTS OF RADIATION OR CYTOSTATIC TREATMENT
16\. A MORE SEVER COUGH (IN THE CASE OF LUNG CANCER
\- excerpt from Defeating Cancer by Gabor Somlyai

Half and half or straight Preventa-25 for 2 days sounds?
.....
one plus two parts=(25+2\*150)/3=108.33 ppm
one plus one parts=(25+150)/2=87.5 ppm
straight =25ppm

So one plus one sounds better now

I was thinking along the same lines. Drinking DDW before sleeping (presuming not eating after sunset), and again at sunrise might allow the DDW to be absorbed as is, as opposed to it "diluting" the deuterium content of any food. I've been wondering if "diluting" the CSF or bloodstream attracts excess deuterium from other tissues to keep the CSF or blood within the "normal" range?

I was thinking that a hibernating bear would be doing a long dry fast, but they are also cold dark and boring, and fat burning, not producing urine or faeces, but recycling their fat store derived DDW, perhaps excreting deuterium via the breath....? Although humans have shrunk hibernation to 2 hours at night, I suppose that technically humans are designed to dry fast overnight ... the longer the hours of darkness, the longer the dry fast .....? so variable depending on latitude and season? And then there's the potential cellular tide changes as a result of the phases of the moon ......  DDW moon phase hack anyone ..?

### Sue-UK — 2018-01-20T14:50:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/deuterium-free-water.20554/post-232958>

> [JanSz said:](/goto/post?id=232762)

> [JanSz said:](/goto/post?id=232762)

I suppose (longer than overnight) dry fasting not being safe for anyone of "advanced age" comes down to heteroplasmy ...? Overnight dry fasting during darkness reminds me of Alexander Wunsch mentioning that Finsen's red room treatment for smallpox was related to subtractive frequencies ..... I suspect, and I'd want to know if sunlight during extended dry fasting could be an issue.

I've also been watching an animated video of the cell organelles moving around the watery cell, extended dry fasting would impact that .... I found this [http://www.mdpi.com/2073-4409/7/1/1/htm](http://www.mdpi.com/2073-4409/7/1/1/htm) _Intra- and Intercellular Quality Control Mechanisms of Mitochondria_. I was thinking in terms of water for the fusion, fission and transmitophagy, and the extracellular vesicles and tunnelling nanotubes....... When my FIL and mum were dying, they first refused food, and then water .... it had me thinking that extended dry fasting could be a last ditch attempt if the situation is dire, or all else has failed, but potentially a big risk as a mitochondrial hack.

I'd also be worried that deuterium in the body isn't necessarily bound with water and in fat tissue ... And if redox is poor in a tissue, even if deuterium is released, there's the potential for it being released from a place of relative safety, (like my fat cells) and finishing up accumulating in my "weakest link" ... I may have misunderstood it  but I thought that drinking DDW helps "rescue" mitochondria by providing light H+..? Extended dry fasting wouldn't provide that ... ?

[https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC4733494/](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC4733494/) _Submolecular regulation of cell transformation by deuterium depleting water exchange reactions in the tricarboxylic acid substrate cycle._ I'm still going through it,  but my first questions are if a cancer cell becomes dehydrated, would its now higher ratio of deuterium in the remaining water promote cell growth or metastasis? Would doing the hack in complete darkness for the 2-3 days make a difference?

Extended dry fasting also seems to break the "redox before detox" rule so at 60 and with my n=1 issues, and with questions piling up ... so far its a no for me .....
